name: Home
title: Home
description: Landing page for accessing water products, dispensers, promotions, search, cart summary, and key site information.

core_features: Initialize homepage data including featured products, featured promotions, search placeholder text, and minimum order information using getHomepageSummary.
Provide a global header with navigation to product categories, deals, help, account, and cart pages, and show the current cart item count using getCartSummary.
Provide a header search form that uses searchProducts for inline search suggestions and redirects to the products listing page with the search term.
Display a grid/list of featured products from getHomepageSummary with navigation to individual product detail pages.
Display a list of featured promotions from getHomepageSummary with navigation to the Deals page.
Show an informational notice about minimum order amount and currency using getHomepageSummary and/or getSiteSettings.
Expose high-level delivery configuration (e.g., available time windows, maximum days ahead) using getSiteSettings for user awareness.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load and data retrieval
1) User opens index.html without needing any URL parameters.
2) The page-shell component triggers getHomepageSummary to retrieve featured_products, featured_promos, search_placeholder, minimum_order_amount, and currency.
3) The page-shell component triggers getCartSummary to retrieve the current cart totals, item_count, applied promo (if any), and minimum_order_amount/currency for the cart context.
4) The page-shell component triggers getSiteSettings to retrieve minimum_order_amount, currency, standard_delivery_time_windows, and max_delivery_schedule_days_ahead.
5) The header-navigation, search-form, featured-product-grid, featured-promotions-list, minimum-order-notice, delivery-info-snippet, and cart-summary-indicator components bind to this data and render their functional content.

Workflow: Navigate to water products from Home
1) User clicks the "Shop Water" navigation element in the header or main navigation area.
2) The header-navigation component constructs a URL products.html?categoryId=water.
3) Browser navigates to products.html with categoryId URL parameter; no additional API calls are made on the Home page.

Workflow: Navigate to dispensers from Home
1) User clicks the "Dispensers & Coolers" navigation element in the header or main navigation area.
2) The header-navigation component constructs a URL products.html?categoryId=dispensers.
3) Browser navigates to products.html with categoryId URL parameter; no additional API calls are made on the Home page.

Workflow: Search for products from the header
1) User types a query into the header search input in the search-form component.
2) On each change (after a debounce), search-form calls searchProducts with parameters: query = current input value, page = 1, pageSize = a dynamically chosen page size, sortBy and sortDirection derived from a default configuration, and filters left empty.
3) search-form receives a list of product results and pagination metadata, and displays a transient suggestion list (e.g., first few product names and/or attributes).
4) User can either select a suggestion (which navigates directly to product_detail.html?id={selectedProductId}) or submit the form.
5) On submit, search-form constructs products.html?q={encodedSearchTerm} and triggers navigation to that URL without using the searchProducts response for the new page content (the products page will handle its own calls).

Workflow: Explore featured products and go to product detail
1) The featured-product-grid component receives featured_products from getHomepageSummary via page-shell.
2) Each product card displays key fields: name, short_description, category_name, water_type_label, size_label, price with currency, rating, rating_count, eco/bpa badges, is_subscription_available, is_rental_product, and image_url.
3) User clicks a product card.
4) featured-product-grid reads the product's id field and navigates to product_detail.html?id={productId}.

Workflow: Explore promotions and go to Deals page
1) The featured-promotions-list component receives featured_promos from getHomepageSummary via page-shell.
2) Each promo item shows name, short_label, description, applicable_order_type, and code (for display only, not for application on this page).
3) User clicks a promo card or a generic "View all deals" action provided within the component.
4) featured-promotions-list triggers navigation to deals.html without additional parameters.

Workflow: View cart summary or go to full cart page
1) The cart-summary-indicator component retrieves cart data from getCartSummary via page-shell.
2) It displays the current item_count and may use total, minimum_order_amount, and currency to show a message indicating the cart progress toward the minimum order.
3) User clicks the cart icon or badge.
4) cart-summary-indicator navigates to cart.html.
5) Optionally, if the URL parameter openCart=1 is present on Home, the cart-summary-indicator may open a read-only dropdown preview bound to getCartSummary.items and totals when the page loads.

Workflow: Access account, help, and informational pages from Home
1) User clicks "My Account" in the header-navigation component.
2) header-navigation navigates to account.html.
3) User clicks "Help" in the header-navigation component.
4) header-navigation navigates to help_center.html.
5) User clicks footer links (About, Contact, My Subscriptions, Upcoming Deliveries) in footer-navigation.
6) footer-navigation navigates respectively to about.html, contact.html, subscriptions.html, and upcoming_deliveries.html.
interactions:
Header navigation clicks:
- Click on the Home link keeps the user on index.html.
- Click on "Shop Water" triggers navigation to products.html?categoryId=water.
- Click on "Dispensers & Coolers" triggers navigation to products.html?categoryId=dispensers.
- Click on "Deals" triggers navigation to deals.html.
- Click on "Help" triggers navigation to help_center.html.
- Click on "My Account" triggers navigation to account.html.

Header search input interactions:
- onInputChange: When the user types into the search input, search-form debounces the input and calls searchProducts with query = current text and default pagination/sort/filter values.
- onSuggestionsResponse: When searchProducts resolves, search-form updates an in-memory suggestions list bound to products from the response.
- onSelectSuggestion: When the user clicks a suggestion item, the component reads the suggestion's id and navigates to product_detail.html?id={id}.
- onSubmit: When the search form is submitted (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking a search button), search-form prevents any default behavior that conflicts with SPA routing and navigates to products.html?q={encoded search term}.

Featured product interactions:
- onClickProductCard: Clicking a product card in featured-product-grid uses the product.id field to navigate to product_detail.html?id={productId}.
- No add-to-cart interactions are defined on this page because no cart mutation interfaces are available; product cards are navigational only.

Featured promotion interactions:
- onClickPromoCard: Clicking a promo card in featured-promotions-list navigates to deals.html.
- onClickViewAllDeals: Clicking a generic "View all deals" link also navigates to deals.html.

Cart indicator interactions:
- onCartSummaryLoad: cart-summary-indicator binds to getCartSummary to display item_count and optionally a progress indicator based on total vs minimum_order_amount.
- onClickCartIcon: Clicking the cart icon navigates to cart.html.
- onToggleCartPreview: If a preview dropdown is implemented, toggling it simply shows/hides the data already retrieved from getCartSummary without performing additional writes.

Minimum order and delivery info interactions:
- minimum-order-notice reads minimum_order_amount and currency either from getHomepageSummary or getSiteSettings and presents them as read-only information; there are no user events that modify these values.
- delivery-info-snippet reads standard_delivery_time_windows and max_delivery_schedule_days_ahead from getSiteSettings and displays them; no user interaction is required other than viewing.

Footer navigation interactions:
- onClickAbout: Navigates to about.html.
- onClickContact: Navigates to contact.html.
- onClickMySubscriptions: Navigates to subscriptions.html.
- onClickUpcomingDeliveries: Navigates to upcoming_deliveries.html.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Home page does not depend on URL parameters for its core data fetching; getHomepageSummary, getCartSummary, and getSiteSettings are called regardless of query string. All product, promo, cart, and site configuration data is sourced dynamically from these interfaces and never hardcoded. URL parameters are only used to control optional UI behaviors on this page and for navigation to other pages.

Navigation state:
- Clicking category links constructs URLs of the form products.html?categoryId=water or products.html?categoryId=dispensers.
- Submitting the header search form constructs URLs of the form products.html?q={encodedSearchTerm}.
- Clicking featured products constructs URLs of the form product_detail.html?id={productId}.
- Clicking cart, account, help, and various footer links navigates to their respective static filenames without additional parameters.

Optional UI parameters on Home:
- openCart: If index.html is loaded with ?openCart=1, the page-shell reads this parameter and informs cart-summary-indicator to open its dropdown preview (if implemented) after getCartSummary resolves. Any other value, or absence of the parameter, keeps the preview closed by default.
- focus: If index.html is loaded with ?focus=search, page-shell instructs search-form to focus the search input on initialization.

Internal state (non-URL):
- search-form maintains in-memory state for the current search input value and the latest suggestions list returned by searchProducts; this state is not persisted to URL parameters or localStorage.
- featured-product-grid and featured-promotions-list maintain the currently loaded lists from getHomepageSummary as in-memory state only.
- cart-summary-indicator stores the latest getCartSummary response in memory for display; it does not alter Cart or CartItem data.

All dynamic values shown to the user (product data, promo details, prices, currency, minimum order amounts, and delivery configuration) are bound directly to the responses from getHomepageSummary, getCartSummary, and getSiteSettings, without any static or hardcoded business values in the Home page implementation.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading for the Home page, orchestrates calls to getHomepageSummary, getCartSummary, and getSiteSettings, manages high-level page state (such as URL-based UI hints), and passes data down as props to child components like header-navigation, search-form, featured-product-grid, featured-promotions-list, minimum-order-notice, cart-summary-indicator, delivery-info-snippet, and footer-navigation.

Provides global navigation links to Home, product category listings (water and dispensers), Deals, Help, My Account, and displays a cart icon with the current item count sourced from cart-summary-indicator/page-shell. It does not fetch data itself but receives the cart item_count from page-shell, which is sourced via getCartSummary.

Renders the global product search input in the header. Uses search_placeholder from getHomepageSummary for the input placeholder text, calls searchProducts to retrieve inline product suggestions as the user types, and on submit redirects the user to the product results page via products.html?q={query}. If the user selects a suggestion, it navigates directly to the corresponding product_detail page.

Displays a functional grid or list of featured products obtained from getHomepageSummary.featured_products. Each product item exposes navigation to its detail page and shows the dynamic attributes provided (name, short_description, category_id/category_name, water_type/water_type_label, size_label, price and currency, rating, rating_count, eco/BPA and other badges, subscription and rental availability, and image_url). It does not modify cart or subscription data.

Presents a list of featured promotions from getHomepageSummary.featured_promos. Each promo entry shows dynamic properties such as name, short_label, description, applicable_order_type, discount_type, discount_value, and code. Interactions on any promo card or on a general "View all deals" control trigger navigation to deals.html.

Shows an informational notice about the current minimum order amount and currency. It binds to minimum_order_amount and currency values sourced from getHomepageSummary (and can fall back or cross-check against getSiteSettings if both are provided), ensuring the message always reflects live configuration. This component is read-only and does not alter any data.

Displays the current cart status using data from getCartSummary: item_count, subtotal, discount_total, total, applied_promo (if any), minimum_order_amount, and currency. Optionally, it can show a progress indicator indicating how close the cart total is to the minimum order amount. The cart icon click navigates to cart.html. If instructed by page-shell (e.g., when URL parameter openCart=1 is present), it may open a preview dropdown showing cart items, but it never modifies cart contents.

Displays high-level delivery-related configuration from getSiteSettings, such as standard_delivery_time_windows and max_delivery_schedule_days_ahead. It transforms the raw identifiers (e.g., '09_00_12_00') into user-readable labels within the component logic, without hardcoding any business rules, and serves purely informational purposes on the Home page.

Provides navigation links to About, Contact, My Subscriptions, and Upcoming Deliveries pages. It does not fetch or display dynamic data by itself but relies on static link behavior and may receive configuration from page-shell if needed (for example, to decide whether certain links should be shown based on SiteSettings, without hardcoding such conditions).

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a strictly top‑to‑bottom layout echoing the mockup: a full‑width header/navigation bar at the very top, a full‑bleed hero region immediately beneath (implemented as part of the page shell), followed by a full‑width white functional band divided into three equal columns containing (from left to right) the search form, the minimum order notice, and the delivery info snippet. Below this band, a main content section presents featured products and promotions side‑by‑side (products taking the dominant left area, promotions on the right), and finally a full‑width footer navigation band at the bottom.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a strictly top‑to‑bottom layout echoing the mockup: a full‑width header/navigation bar at the very top, a full‑bleed hero region immediately beneath (implemented as part of the page shell), followed by a full‑width white functional band divided into three equal columns containing (from left to right) the search form, the minimum order notice, and the delivery info snippet. Below this band, a main content section presents featured products and promotions side‑by‑side (products taking the dominant left area, promotions on the right), and finally a full‑width footer navigation band at the bottom.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: On initial load, the header occupies a shallow strip (~10–15% of the viewport height), the hero image region beneath it dominates the upper fold (~40–50%), the three‑column functional band below takes roughly ~20–25%, and the remaining vertical space on larger screens is given to the featured product and promotion content. Horizontally, a 12‑column grid guides layout: header content sits within a centered max‑width container; the three‑column band assigns roughly 4 columns each to search, notice, and delivery info; the product grid later claims about 8 columns (≈2/3 width) with promotions in the remaining 4 columns (≈1/3 width). Generous gutters separate columns to preserve the spacious feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderately spacious density: navigation items have comfortable horizontal padding; the hero area is visually open with large imagery. Within the three‑column band, inputs and text blocks have 16–24px vertical spacing and ample side padding so fields do not feel crowded. The product grid presents a manageable number of cards per row (typically 3–4 on desktop) with consistent 16–24px gaps. Overall section padding uses 24–48px vertical spacing based on an 8px scale, ensuring clear separation between sections without feeling sparse.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve the mockup’s Z‑pattern and top‑to‑bottom flow: the eye moves from the top‑left logo across the navigation to the high‑contrast ORDER button and subtle cart summary on the top right, then drops into the center of the hero image. From there, the eye continues downward to the three‑column band, scanning naturally from left (search form as the primary interaction) to middle (minimum order notice) to right (delivery info). After this, the gaze descends into the product grid, scanning across rows of products and finally to the promotions column on the right, before ending at the structured footer navigation at the bottom.

layout.overall_description: The page reads as a clean, corporate landing experience with a strong top bar, a photographic hero, and a functional band that mirrors an order/contact strip. A white header with the logo on the left, navigation in the center, and a bright orange ORDER button plus a compact cart indicator on the right is fixed to the top, framing the experience. Below, a full‑bleed hero image of a water bottle and office scene stretches edge‑to‑edge, occupying nearly half the initial viewport and establishing brand context. Overlapping the bottom edge of this hero or sitting directly beneath it is a white three‑column band, spanning the full width of the content area: on the left, a stacked search form helps users quickly find water products or dispensers; in the center, a clearly framed minimum‑order notice gives essential policy information; on the right, a concise delivery info snippet lists hours and service coverage in line‑broken text, echoing the contact hours block in the mockup. Beneath this band, the main content uses a two‑column grid: a broader left column for the featured product grid and a narrower right column for a vertical list of promotions, all aligned to the same content width. The page ends with a structured footer navigation bar, full‑width but content‑bounded, providing secondary links and site information while visually anchoring the layout. The consistent use of the 12‑column grid, generous gutters, and 8px‑based spacing system gives the entire page a modern, orderly, and professional feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page shell acts as the full‑page canvas and grid framework. It defines a full‑width background with a light, neutral base color and a centered content container of approximately 1120–1200px max width that all inner sections align to. At the top, it reserves a shallow horizontal strip for the header (roughly 72–96px tall), followed immediately by a full‑bleed hero image region that spans the entire viewport width and about 40–50% of the viewport height on desktop. The core content container sits vertically centered within this hero region to position focal imagery near the center. Directly below (or slightly overlapping the bottom of the hero via negative margin) the shell hosts a full‑width white band that uses a 12‑column grid to divide content into three equal columns for the search form, minimum order notice, and delivery info snippet. Below this band, the main content area is arranged as a single large section where the left 8 columns are dedicated to the featured product grid and the right 4 columns to the featured promotions list. At the very bottom, the shell provides a full‑width footer navigation band. Vertical spacing between these sections follows an 8px scale, with 32–48px padding above and below major sections to echo the mockup’s spaciousness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The page shell is a standard scrollable document root that manages vertical flow. It maintains fixed side margins for the central content container and allows the header (contained within it) to remain sticky while other sections scroll naturally underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header navigation forms a full‑width horizontal bar pinned to the top of the viewport. It has a modest height (approximately 72–88px) and a solid white or very light background, creating a clean frame against which the hero imagery scrolls behind or beneath it. Within the centered max‑width content container, a three‑part horizontal layout is used: on the far left, the logo sits flush with the left padding; in the middle, a row of navigation links stretches horizontally with equal spacing, aligned along the vertical center of the bar; on the right, the cart summary indicator is positioned just to the left of a high‑contrast orange ORDER button. The ORDER button sits near the right edge, with about 24–32px of right padding before the viewport edge, creating a strong visual anchor in the top‑right. Vertical padding around the nav items and buttons is generous (roughly 16–20px), ensuring the header feels airy rather than cramped. The entire header spans the full page width but constrains interactive elements to the central content width to maintain alignment with the sections below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky: the header bar remains fixed at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls, ensuring persistent access to navigation, the ORDER call‑to‑action, and the cart summary indicator.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search form is positioned in the left column of the three‑column white band directly beneath the hero image, mirroring the ‘customer type’ and ‘name’ column in the mockup’s order area. This band stretches full width across the page but constrains its internal content to the central grid. The search form occupies roughly the left 4 columns (≈33%) of this grid, starting at the left edge of the content container. Vertically, it sits near the top of the band with 24–32px padding above and below. Within this column, the search field and any supporting controls (e.g., a product category dropdown) are stacked vertically, left‑aligned, with approximately 16–24px of vertical spacing between them. The main search input spans nearly the full width of this column, leaving a consistent 16–24px internal horizontal padding within the input itself and 16–24px padding between the input’s outer edge and the column’s boundary. The submit button (if separate) is placed immediately to the right of the main search input or stacked beneath it depending on available width, maintaining a balanced, uncluttered look. On smaller screens, this column will stack above the others and the search form will expand toward full width while preserving 16–24px of side padding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within its section: the search form scrolls with the three‑column band but is visually anchored as the leftmost, first‑scan element immediately under the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured product grid sits in the main content section directly below the three‑column band, occupying the dominant left portion of a two‑column layout. Within the 12‑column grid, it spans approximately 8 columns (roughly 2/3 of the content width) starting from the left margin of the central container. A section heading (e.g., “Featured Water & Dispensers”) is placed at the top‑left of this area with 24–32px of padding above, followed by the grid of product cards. Cards are arranged in rows of 3 (or 4, depending on final breakpoints) with consistent 16–24px gaps both horizontally and vertically, creating a neat, balanced matrix. Each card occupies one grid column in this 8‑column area, with equal spacing to its neighbors and the container edges. The entire product grid block is vertically aligned with the promotions list to its right so that both sections start at the same vertical baseline, reinforcing horizontal alignment. There is 32–48px of vertical space between this section and the three‑column band above, and a similar buffer before the footer below, keeping the layout airy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the main content flow: the product grid scrolls naturally as part of the page content, maintaining its relative position to the promotions list on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured promotions list shares a horizontal band with the product grid, positioned to the right as a narrower companion column. It occupies approximately the rightmost 4 columns of the 12‑column grid (about 1/3 of the content width). The top of this promotions column aligns vertically with the top of the featured product grid’s heading to emphasize a cohesive row. A small section title (e.g., “Promotions” or “Special Offers”) appears at the top, followed by a vertical stack of promotion cards or tiles. Each promo card spans nearly the full width of this right column with 16–24px padding inside, and 16–20px of vertical separation between cards. The list height may extend beyond the product grid content depending on the number of promotions; in that case, the extra height simply scrolls, maintaining a clear right‑hand strip of promotional content. The column is separated from the product grid by a visible gutter of approximately 24–32px to avoid crowding and to visually distinguish primary products from secondary offers.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content row: it remains right‑aligned to the grid and scrolls with the page, complementing but not overtaking the product grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The minimum order notice is placed in the central column of the three‑column white band directly under the hero, mirroring the middle column of inputs in the original order/contact section. It occupies roughly the middle 4 columns (≈33%) of the internal grid, centered between the search form on the left and the delivery info snippet on the right. Horizontally, it is left‑aligned within its column, but its content can be center‑weighted visually by using a short heading line followed by one or two lines of explanatory text. The text block is vertically aligned with the top of the search form, with 24–32px padding above and below to create a sense of balance in the band. A subtle icon or label (e.g., “Minimum Order”) can precede the text, and line spacing between the heading and detail lines should follow the 8px scale (roughly 8–12px) to keep the content legible and not dense. This central column acts as a passive informational anchor that the eye naturally passes through when scanning from left to right across the band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the three‑column band: it stays centered as the middle column and scrolls out of view with the band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary indicator is integrated into the header navigation’s right side, positioned just to the left of the orange ORDER button. It appears as a compact icon (e.g., a cart or bottle crate) possibly accompanied by a small numeric badge indicating item count. Vertically, it is centered within the header bar, aligning with the navigation links and ORDER button. Horizontally, there is a small, consistent gap (about 12–16px) between the cart indicator and the ORDER button so they feel related but distinct. The indicator itself is relatively small compared to the ORDER button, maintaining a tertiary visual weight while still being easily discoverable at a glance. Its alignment follows the header’s right padding, ensuring that as the viewport changes, it remains anchored in the top‑right area alongside primary actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It inherits the header’s sticky behavior, remaining visible at the top‑right of the screen as the user scrolls, but does not float independently of the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery info snippet occupies the rightmost column of the three‑column white band directly beneath the hero, echoing the right column in the original mockup that lists customer service hours and a phone number. It takes up approximately the right 4 columns of the 12‑column grid (≈33% of the band). The content is arranged as a stack of short text lines—such as delivery hours, coverage areas, and a key contact number—aligned left within this column but visually grouped to form a compact block. A subtle heading (e.g., “Delivery Info”) sits at the top, followed by one or two short lines of detail, each separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing. The entire block is vertically aligned with the top of the search form and minimum order notice to maintain a consistent row across the band. This right column naturally becomes the final stop in the user’s lateral scan of the band, providing contextual reassurance (when we deliver, where we deliver) after they see search and policy information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the three‑column band: it stays anchored to the right side of the content container and scrolls normally with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer navigation forms the final full‑width horizontal band at the bottom of the page. It uses a slightly darker or more muted background than the main content to visually separate itself, but maintains the same centered max‑width content container and alignment as the header and main sections. Within this container, links are organized into 2–4 vertical columns (e.g., ‘Company’, ‘Support’, ‘Services’, ‘Legal’) evenly distributed across the width, each column occupying roughly 3–4 grid columns depending on count. The columns are left‑aligned with consistent 16–24px vertical spacing between link items. The footer has 32–48px of vertical padding above and below its content, giving it a grounded, substantial presence. A small, subtle bottom row may run full width within the container for copyright text and perhaps minimal social icons, aligned either center or split with text left and icons right, while still respecting the central grid alignment. The footer visually anchors the page, mirroring the structured feel of the header but with lower visual intensity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the end of the document flow: it appears once the user scrolls to the bottom and does not stick or float.

name: Product Listing
title: Products
description: Browse and search bottled water and dispenser products with filters, sorting, and pagination. The page uses URL parameters and backend interfaces to load and refine product listings.

core_features: Determine page mode (category listing vs search results) from URL parameters and initialize data loading.

Load available filter options for the active category using getProductFilterOptions and render filter controls (size, water type, packaging, price range, rating, eco-friendly, BPA-free, case/rental/subscription availability).
List products for the current category with listProducts or search across products with searchProducts, using URL-driven filters, sorting, and pagination.
Allow users to adjust filters and sort options; changes update URL parameters, reset pagination appropriately, and trigger new listProducts or searchProducts calls.
Display applied filters (including the search query) and allow users to remove individual filters or clear all filters, synchronizing with URL state.
Provide pagination controls that navigate between result pages by updating the page URL parameter and reloading data.
Allow navigation to product_detail.html when a product card is clicked by passing the product’s id as a URL parameter.
Support navigation to cart.html via the header cart icon and to index.html via the site logo without affecting listing state.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Browse a category and refine with filters
1. User opens products.html with a categoryId parameter and no q parameter.
2. Page controller parses URL parameters (categoryId, page, sortBy, sortDirection, and all filter parameters).
3. Page controller calls getProductFilterOptions with categoryId and passes the result to the filter-sidebar.
4. Page controller builds a filters object from URL parameters and calls listProducts with categoryId, page, pageSize, sortBy, sortDirection, and filters.
5. Product list and pagination are rendered using the listProducts response.
6. User adjusts filters in the filter-sidebar; each change updates the corresponding URL parameters (and resets page to the first page), then triggers a new listProducts call.
7. User optionally changes sort options; sortBy and sortDirection URL parameters are updated, page resets, and listProducts is called again.

Workflow: Search for products and refine results
1. User submits a search (e.g., from a global header search) and is taken to products.html with a q parameter and optional categoryId and filter/sort parameters.
2. Page controller parses q and other URL parameters and determines search mode (q is present).
3. If categoryId is present, page controller may call getProductFilterOptions with that categoryId to populate filters; otherwise, filter-sidebar can be limited or hidden depending on configuration.
4. Page controller builds a filters object from URL parameters and calls searchProducts with query=q, page, pageSize, sortBy, sortDirection, and filters.
5. Product list, pagination, and search context (showing the query) are rendered from searchProducts results.
6. User adjusts filters or sort options; URL parameters are updated, page resets to the first page, and searchProducts is called again with the updated filters and sorting.

Workflow: Change page of results
1. User is viewing a product list with existing filters and sorting.
2. User interacts with the pagination-control to go to a different page (next, previous, or a specific page number).
3. Pagination-control updates the page parameter in the URL while preserving categoryId, q, sortBy, sortDirection, and filter parameters.
4. Page controller detects the URL change and calls listProducts or searchProducts (depending on mode) with the new page.
5. product-grid updates to show products for the newly requested page.

Workflow: Clear or remove filters
1. User has active filters applied via URL parameters.
2. active-filters-bar shows each applied filter as a removable chip along with an option to clear all filters.
3. When the user removes a single filter chip, the corresponding URL parameter is updated to remove that filter value; page is reset to the first page, and listProducts or searchProducts is called again.
4. When the user chooses to clear all filters, filter-related URL parameters are removed, page is reset to the first page, and products are reloaded with an empty filters object.

Workflow: View a product’s details
1. User sees a list of products in product-grid.
2. User clicks on a product card.
3. product-card triggers navigation to product_detail.html with id set to the clicked product’s id.
4. The product listing state remains encoded in the URL on products.html, so using the browser back button returns the user to the same filtered and paginated listing.
interactions: On initial page load or when the browser URL changes (including back/forward), the page controller parses URL parameters to determine mode (category vs search) and triggers the appropriate data-loading sequence.
Filter-sidebar displays filter controls based on getProductFilterOptions output and the current applied filters derived from URL parameters, allowing users to toggle, select, or adjust filters.
Filter adjustments in filter-sidebar (e.g., selecting/deselecting options or changing numeric ranges) update the corresponding URL parameters and notify the page controller to reload products.

Sort-control allows the user to select a sort option; each option maps to a combination of sortBy and sortDirection URL parameters, which are updated and used in subsequent listProducts or searchProducts calls.
active-filters-bar shows the current filters (including search query if present) and supports interaction to remove individual filters or clear all filters, directly manipulating URL parameters.

product-grid renders a list of product-card components, each bound to data from listProducts.products or searchProducts.products; clicking anywhere on a product-card triggers navigation to product_detail.html with the product id.
pagination-control displays pagination using the pagination object returned by listProducts or searchProducts; user interactions update the page URL parameter and reload results.

search-form (when used on this page) submits a search query, updating the q parameter in the URL (and optionally categoryId and filter/sort parameters) and switching to search mode which uses searchProducts.
Site logo click navigates to index.html; cart icon click navigates to cart.html; these interactions do not call listing-related interfaces but are part of the page’s navigation behavior.
Empty-state component displays when no products are returned and offers actions such as clearing filters; these actions adjust URL parameters and trigger a new listProducts or searchProducts call.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page’s state is entirely derived from and written to URL parameters. The primary mode is determined as follows: if the q parameter is present and non-empty, the page operates in search mode and uses searchProducts; otherwise, it operates in category listing mode and uses listProducts. In category mode, categoryId must be present and is passed to both getProductFilterOptions and listProducts. In search mode, q is passed to searchProducts; categoryId is optional and, when present, is passed to getProductFilterOptions to provide category-specific filter options.

On any load or URL change, the page controller reads these parameters: categoryId, q, page, sortBy, sortDirection, size, waterType, packaging, priceMin, priceMax, ratingMin, ecoFriendly, bpaFree, isCase, isRental, isSubscriptionAvailable, isOneTimePurchaseAvailable. The controller constructs a filters object with properties matching the filters structure expected by listProducts and searchProducts (e.g., mapping size to sizeFilterKeys, waterType to waterTypes, and so on). Boolean parameters are interpreted according to their presence and value in the query string. The page parameter defaults to the first page when not specified or when filters/sorting are changed.

For category listings, the controller calls getProductFilterOptions with the categoryId to retrieve available filter options (including size_options, water_type_options, packaging_category_options, price_range, rating_options, and flags indicating availability of eco-friendly, BPA-free, rental, and subscription filters). These results inform which filter controls are rendered and how they are constrained. The same categoryId, along with page, sortBy, sortDirection, pageSize, and the constructed filters object, is passed to listProducts. listProducts returns products, pagination information, and appliedFilters, which may be used to validate or normalize the filters reflected in the UI. In search mode, the controller skips listProducts and instead calls searchProducts with query=q, page, pageSize, sortBy, sortDirection, and the same filters object. searchProducts returns products and pagination only, so applied filters in the UI are derived wholly from URL parameters in this mode.

Whenever a user interaction changes filters, sort options, or pagination, the corresponding URL parameters are updated. For filters and sorting, page is reset to the first page. The URL update uses navigation (e.g., modifying the query string) rather than localStorage, and the page controller responds to this URL change by re-parsing parameters and issuing fresh getProductFilterOptions/listProducts or searchProducts calls as appropriate. This ensures that the browser’s back/forward buttons correctly restore prior listing states, and that the URL can be shared to reproduce the exact same results view.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page load, determines whether to use category listing or search results, parses URL parameters into structured state, calls getProductFilterOptions and either listProducts or searchProducts, and distributes response data to child components. Listens for URL changes and child component events that require URL updates and data reloads.

Displays context information for the current listing, such as the category name or search query and total number of results. In category mode, derives the category display name from one of the returned products or category-related data; in search mode, shows the q parameter and may indicate the number of results from pagination.totalItems.

Provides an input control for users to enter a search query directly on the products page. On submit, updates the q parameter in the URL (and resets page and filter-related parameters as configured) to switch the page into search mode and invoke searchProducts via the page-controller.

Renders filter controls for size, water type, packaging category, price range, rating, eco-friendly, BPA-free, case products, rental products, and subscription / one-time purchase availability, based on the output of getProductFilterOptions and the current filter URL parameters. When the user changes any filter, it constructs updated query parameter values and passes them to the page-controller to update the URL and reload products.

Displays a consolidated view of currently applied filters and the search query as removable chips or tokens. Uses URL parameters (and appliedFilters from listProducts when available) to determine what is active. Removing a chip or clearing all filters triggers URL updates and product reloads via the page-controller.

Allows users to select a sorting option for the product listing. Each selection maps to specific sortBy and sortDirection URL parameter values. When the user changes the sort option, sort-control notifies the page-controller to update the URL (including resetting the page parameter) and reload products using the selected sort configuration.

Displays a grid or list of products returned from either listProducts or searchProducts. Instantiates a product-card component for each product in the response. It is purely a view-level component and does not directly call interfaces; instead, it reacts to data provided by the page-controller.

Represents a single product entry in the product-grid. Displays product information such as name, description snippet, price, size, packaging type, rating, eco/BPA flags, badges, and rental/subscription availability as provided in the Product fields. When clicked, it navigates to product_detail.html with the product’s id in the query string.

Displays pagination controls based on the pagination object from listProducts or searchProducts. It allows users to move between pages. When a user selects a page or navigates next/previous, it updates the page parameter in the URL and triggers the page-controller to fetch the relevant page of products.

Handles the case when no products are returned by listProducts or searchProducts (totalItems is zero). It displays a message and may offer actions such as clearing filters. When the user chooses to clear filters, it triggers removal of filter-related URL parameters and reloads the listing via the page-controller.
Shows and hides loading feedback while getProductFilterOptions, listProducts, or searchProducts requests are in progress. The page-controller toggles its state based on request lifecycle.

Provides navigation links including the site logo, category navigation (e.g., links that set categoryId on products.html), and the cart icon. Clicking the logo navigates to index.html. Clicking the cart icon navigates to cart.html. Category navigation links route to products.html with appropriate URL parameters, which the page-controller then uses to initialize filters and product listing.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a clear, top-to-bottom layout that mirrors the mockup: a full-width header-nav bar at the very top, followed by a full-bleed listing-header hero section that sets context for the product listing. Directly beneath the hero, a full-width control band houses the search-form at the center with the active-filters-bar and sort-control aligned just below it as a horizontal control row. Below this control band, the page splits into a two-column content area: a left filter-sidebar column and a right product-grid column. The pagination-control sits at the bottom of the product area. The empty-state and loading-indicator occupy the same spatial region as the product-grid when applicable. The page-controller is a non-visual layer that orchestrates URL parameters and state but does not affect physical layout.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a clear, top-to-bottom layout that mirrors the mockup: a full-width header-nav bar at the very top, followed by a full-bleed listing-header hero section that sets context for the product listing. Directly beneath the hero, a full-width control band houses the search-form at the center with the active-filters-bar and sort-control aligned just below it as a horizontal control row. Below this control band, the page splits into a two-column content area: a left filter-sidebar column and a right product-grid column. The pagination-control sits at the bottom of the product area. The empty-state and loading-indicator occupy the same spatial region as the product-grid when applicable. The page-controller is a non-visual layer that orchestrates URL parameters and state but does not affect physical layout.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header-nav occupies a shallow horizontal strip, about 10–15% of the first viewport height. The listing-header hero section takes the visual lead, around 40–50% of the initial viewport height, spanning full width. The search + controls band just below the hero uses roughly 15–20% of the initial viewport height, with generous vertical padding to echo the mockup’s order/contact band. The remaining space on the page is dedicated to the main content: the filter-sidebar occupies about 25–30% of the content width (3–4 of 12 columns) and the product-grid occupies the remaining 70–75% (8–9 of 12 columns). Vertical spacing between major bands (hero to search, search to main content, grid to pagination) follows the 32–48px rhythm via section padding. Within each component, controls follow the 8px-based scale for internal padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a moderate, breathable density: no cramped controls, generous horizontal gutters, and comfortable vertical spacing between inputs and cards. Interactive controls like search-form, active-filters-bar, and sort-control are grouped but not stacked too tightly—roughly 16–24px between rows and 8–16px between related elements. The filter-sidebar groups filter blocks with clear separation. Product cards are sizable and easy to scan, with ample negative space around images, text, and CTAs. The layout feels like a professional service site rather than a dense ecommerce dashboard.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern similar to the mockup: the user starts at the top-left logo in the header-nav, scans right across navigation items to the high-contrast CTA button, then drops down into the center-left of the listing-header hero (page title and contextual imagery). From there, the eye moves downward to the centered search-form in the control band, then continues across the row of active filters and the sort-control on the right. Below that, the left-hand filter-sidebar anchors the eye, but attention quickly shifts right to the product-grid where cards are scanned left-to-right, top-to-bottom. The flow concludes at the pagination-control at the bottom of the grid. Active colors (aqua + orange) emphasize primary actions like search and ordering without overwhelming the calm, corporate photography in the hero.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a polished, corporate product listing for a water delivery service. At the very top, a slim, full-width navigation bar with the brand logo on the left, horizontal menu items across the center, and a standout orange “Order” button on the right anchors the experience. Directly below, a full-bleed hero (listing-header) shows a professional office scene with water bottles in the foreground, occupying nearly half of the first viewport and providing context for the catalog. Over this hero or aligned in its lower portion, a clear title like “Products” and a short subtitle set expectations. Beneath the hero, a white control band stretches edge to edge, echoing the mockup’s order/contact strip: at its top, a large, centered search-form lets users search products; directly under it, a horizontal row aligns the active-filters-bar to the left and the sort-control to the right, spaced with comfortable breathing room. The page then transitions into a two-column content area: on the left, a vertically stacked filter-sidebar occupying roughly one quarter of the width, and on the right, a spacious product-grid with three columns of product-cards on desktop. Cards are evenly spaced with generous gutters, maintaining an airy feel. At the bottom of the grid, a centered pagination-control sits with ample top margin, clearly marking the end of the listing. When no products match, a clean empty-state replaces the grid content; while loading, a subtle overlay loading-indicator covers the grid area. Throughout, sections are separated by consistent 32–48px vertical padding, preserving the mockup’s spacious rhythm and guiding the user’s eyes smoothly from navigation, to hero, to search and filters, and finally to the products themselves.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-nav is a full-width horizontal bar fixed to the very top of the page, spanning 100% of the viewport width and approximately 72–96px in height (around 10–15% of the initial viewport). It uses a solid, light background color to contrast with the hero image below. The logo sits at the far left with a small horizontal offset (~24px from the viewport edge). To the right of the logo, main navigation links are arranged in a single horizontal row, each with 16–24px horizontal padding, centered vertically in the bar. On the far right, aligned flush with the right margin (~24px from the edge), an orange CTA button (e.g., “ORDER”) stands out as the most visually prominent element in the header. The header-nav stretches edge-to-edge with no visible border but may cast a subtle bottom shadow to distinguish it from the hero below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky at the top of the viewport on scroll (fixed to top), remaining visible while the rest of the page content scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The listing-header occupies the full width of the page immediately below the header-nav. It presents as a full-bleed hero section with a photographic background (office with water bottles), taking up roughly 40–50% of the initial viewport height. The focal point of the imagery is slightly left of center, aligning with the mockup’s composition. Overlaid on this hero, the page title (e.g., “Products” or “Bottled Water & Dispensers”) is placed left-of-center horizontally and vertically centered within the hero’s safe area, with a short descriptive subtitle beneath it. Text is set on a subtle translucent overlay or gradient to ensure readability against the photo. The bottom of this hero aligns closely with the top of the search-form band, with about 32–40px of vertical breathing room or an intentional overlap where the search-form card slightly intrudes into the hero to create visual connection, mirroring the mockup’s hero-to-form transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; does not stick, but due to height it dominates the initial viewport below the fixed header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-form is placed directly below the listing-header hero as the first element in a white control band that stretches full width. It is centered horizontally on the page and spans approximately 60–70% of the content width, leaving equal left and right margins that align with the underlying 12-column grid. The form itself is a single horizontal row on desktop: a large search input taking up roughly 75–80% of the form’s width on the left, and a prominent aqua or orange search button occupying the remaining 20–25% on the right, both with generous internal padding (10–12px vertical, 16–20px horizontal). There is 24–32px of padding above and below the search-form inside this band, visually separating it from the active-filters-bar and sort-control below. On narrower viewports, the search input and button stack vertically with 8–12px vertical spacing but remain centered within the same width constraints.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band, centered and responsive; it remains at the top of the main content but scrolls out of view as the user moves down the listing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar begins a short distance (about 24–32px) below the control band (search-form, active-filters-bar, sort-control) and is fixed to the left side of the main content area. On desktop, it occupies roughly 25–30% of the page width (about 3–4 columns of a 12-column grid), starting at the left content margin that aligns with the search-form’s left edge. The sidebar extends downward for the length of the filter content, creating a solid vertical block that visually anchors the page. Inside, filter groups are stacked vertically with 24–32px separation between groups and 8–12px spacing between controls within a group, maintaining a moderate density. The top of the sidebar aligns vertically with the top row of the product-grid to its right, so both start at the same horizontal line. On tablet or smaller screens, the filter-sidebar collapses into an expandable drawer or full-width section positioned above the product-grid, but still visually grouped just below the search and control band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the filter-sidebar can behave as a sticky column that remains visible within the viewport as the user scrolls the products (sticking within its containing column while respecting page footer boundaries). On mobile, it typically becomes a slide-out or collapsible section that overlays or precedes the product-grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active-filters-bar sits directly below the search-form within the same white control band, forming the left side of a horizontal row that spans the full content width above the main product area. It is left-aligned with the main content column (same left margin as filter-sidebar and product-grid) and occupies roughly 65–70% of the row’s width, leaving room for the sort-control on the right. Active filter chips are laid out horizontally, wrapping to multiple lines if necessary, with 8px gaps between chips and 8–12px vertical padding within the bar. There is about 12–16px vertical spacing between the bottom of the search-form and the top of this bar, and an additional 16–24px from the bottom of the bar to the beginning of the filter-sidebar/product-grid section, giving a clear three-layered stack: hero → search → active filters/sort → grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band but may be implemented as sticky within the main content area on longer product lists, so that the active-filters-bar remains pinned to the top of the viewport while the grid scrolls beneath it, improving clarity of applied filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control shares the same horizontal row as the active-filters-bar within the control band. It is aligned to the far right of the content area, flush with the right margin that matches the product-grid’s right edge. On desktop, it occupies approximately 30–35% of the row’s width, presenting as a compact, right-aligned dropdown or segmented control. The sort label (e.g., “Sort by”) sits left of the dropdown, with 8px spacing between the label and the interactive element. Vertically, it is centered with the active-filters-bar, creating a clean, balanced row. There is 16–24px spacing below this row before the main content (filter-sidebar and product-grid) begins. On tablet and mobile, the sort-control stacks below the active-filters-bar and becomes full-width, but it maintains a slightly tighter visual footprint to stay unobtrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band. If the active-filters-bar row becomes sticky, the sort-control sticks alongside it, keeping sorting options accessible as the user scrolls the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid is the dominant content area to the right of the filter-sidebar. It begins on the same horizontal line as the top of the filter-sidebar and extends to the right content margin, occupying roughly 70–75% of the page width (about 8–9 of 12 columns). On desktop, product cards are arranged in a 3-column grid with equal column widths and consistent horizontal gutters of 24–32px between columns. Vertically, rows are separated by 24–32px as well, matching the overall spacious rhythm. The grid has 8–16px padding at its top to separate the first row of cards from the active-filters/sort band above, and 32–40px padding at the bottom before the pagination-control. On tablet, the grid shifts to 2 columns; on mobile, it becomes a single column centered within the same content margins. The overall grid area is visually balanced against the filter-sidebar, creating a stable two-column layout reminiscent of a service-oriented product catalog.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolling within the page. During loading, it is visually overlaid by the loading-indicator without shifting layout; during empty states, the grid content is replaced while the surrounding spacing (top and bottom margins) is preserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-card is a white, rectangular tile within the product-grid that fits cleanly into its column. Cards have a subtle border or light shadow and rounded corners consistent with a modern corporate aesthetic. They maintain a roughly 3:4 aspect ratio (slightly taller than wide) on desktop. Inside each card, the top half is dominated by the product image centered horizontally, with 16–24px padding around it. Below the image, the product name appears in bold, left-aligned text, followed by price and key details with 8–12px vertical spacing. At the bottom, a full-width CTA button (e.g., “Order” or “Add to Delivery”) in the brand’s orange spans the card’s width with 10–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal insets. Internally, the card uses a consistent 16–24px padding from edges to content, and between text elements, keeping everything legible and airy. All cards in a row align to the same height where possible, creating a clean horizontal rhythm across the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the product-grid; cards reflow responsively as columns change count (3 → 2 → 1) based on viewport width, preserving consistent internal padding and external gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-control is placed directly below the product-grid, aligned with the grid’s width. It is horizontally centered within the grid column, with approximately 32–48px of vertical space separating it from the last row of product cards, making it clearly the final element in the listing. Controls (page numbers, next/previous arrows) are arranged in a horizontal line, using 8–12px spacing between buttons and 8–10px internal padding. The current page is highlighted with a stronger aqua or orange accent. The overall height of the pagination area is modest, but the generous top margin and centered positioning give it sufficient visual prominence without competing with the hero or search areas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the product listing area; as the user navigates pages, the content above updates while the pagination-control itself remains in the same relative position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state occupies the same spatial region as the product-grid when no products are available. It is centered within the right content column (aligned with the grid’s left and right boundaries) and uses a vertically stacked arrangement. At the top, an illustrative icon or simple line illustration is centered horizontally, followed by a prominent heading (e.g., “No products found”) and a secondary explanatory line of text. Below this, a CTA (such as “Clear filters” or “View all products”) is centered and styled as a medium-emphasis button. Vertical spacing between these elements follows a 16–24px rhythm, and the entire empty state is padded with 48–64px of top and bottom whitespace within the grid area, giving it a calm, uncluttered appearance. The filter-sidebar remains visible to the left, reinforcing that the user can adjust filters to change the state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the product-grid content within its column while preserving the overall layout: filter-sidebar remains in place, control band above stays visible, and pagination-control is hidden when the empty-state is active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears over the product-grid area (and empty-state if applicable) as a semi-transparent overlay that dimly veils the cards while they load. It covers the entire grid region from the same top alignment as the first row of products down to just above the pagination-control area, without affecting the filter-sidebar or the control band above. At the center of this overlay, horizontally and vertically, a circular spinner or subtle animated icon is displayed, sized modestly but clearly visible. The rest of the page (header-nav, listing-header, search-form, filters) remains visible and static in the background, maintaining the page’s overall structure while indicating that results are being refreshed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned absolutely over the product-grid area with no impact on layout; it appears and disappears without shifting content, blocking interaction with the grid while active but allowing scrolling and filter changes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-controller is a non-visual, logical component associated with the main content area starting just below the search and control band. Conceptually, it spans the width of the content area that includes the filter-sidebar, product-grid, active-filters-bar, sort-control, pagination-control, empty-state, and loading-indicator. It does not render any visible UI but governs the synchronization of URL parameters, filter states, sorting, search queries, and pagination. Spatially, it can be thought of as an invisible layer behind the main content column that tracks user interactions and triggers updates to the visible components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; it does not occupy space or affect layout flow. It passively listens to interactions and orchestrates state changes for the rest of the components.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays full details for a single product, lets the user choose purchase mode (one-time, subscription, or rental), configure options, and add the configured item to the cart while showing current cart totals.

core_features: Load and display full product information for the product specified by the URL parameter `id` using `getProductDetails`.
Show available purchase modes (one-time purchase, subscription, rental) based on `purchase_options` from `getProductDetails` and the product flags.
Allow users to configure quantity, subscription frequency, subscription delivery day, or rental duration using the configuration options returned by `getProductDetails`.
Validate the current configuration and add the selected product configuration to the cart by calling `addItemToCart`.
Display an up-to-date cart summary (item count, totals, applied promo, minimum order amount) using `getCartSummary` and update it after items are added.
Provide navigation to the cart page, home page, and back to the product listing using the defined outgoing connections and URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View product details:
1. User arrives at `product_detail.html?id={productId}` from another page.
2. On page initialization, the page reads the `id` URL parameter.
3. The page calls `getProductDetails(productId)`.
4. While data is loading, a loading state is shown; no purchase actions are enabled.
5. When data is returned, the page displays the product image, name, description, size, type, packaging category, rating, rating count, and any badges/eco indicators from the `product` object.
6. The page also reads `purchase_options` to determine which purchase modes and configuration fields to show.

Configure and add a one-time purchase:
1. After product data is loaded, the user selects the one-time purchase mode if available (or it is selected by default based on `purchase_options.can_one_time`).
2. The user adjusts the quantity using a quantity input initialized from `purchase_options.default_quantity`.
3. The page validates that quantity is a positive value according to business rules.
4. The user clicks an "Add to Cart" action for the one-time configuration.
5. The page calls `addItemToCart` with `itemType = 'one_time'`, passing the current `productId` and `quantity`. Subscription and rental-related parameters are omitted or left undefined.
6. On success, the page reads the returned `cart` object, updates the cart summary widget, and shows the success feedback using the returned `message`.
7. The user can then click a "View Cart" action or the cart icon to navigate to `cart.html`.

Configure and add a subscription:
1. The user selects the subscription mode if `purchase_options.can_subscription` is true and the product supports subscriptions.
2. The page shows subscription configuration fields: quantity, subscription frequency, and delivery day of week.
3. The frequency dropdown is populated from `purchase_options.subscription_frequency_options`, and the delivery day dropdown is populated from `purchase_options.delivery_day_options`.
4. The user selects a frequency and a delivery day, and sets the desired quantity.
5. Before adding to cart, the page validates that quantity is valid and that both a frequency and delivery day have been selected.
6. The user clicks the "Add to Cart" action for the subscription.
7. The page calls `addItemToCart` with `itemType = 'subscription'`, passing `productId`, `quantity`, `subscriptionFrequency` and `subscriptionDeliveryDayOfWeek` based on the selected option values.
8. On success, the page updates the cart summary using the `cart` object returned by `addItemToCart` and shows the returned `message` to confirm the subscription item was added.

Configure and add a rental:
1. If `purchase_options.can_rental` is true and the product is a rental product, the user selects the rental mode.
2. The page displays rental configuration fields: quantity and rental duration.
3. The rental duration dropdown is populated from `purchase_options.rental_duration_options`.
4. The user selects a rental duration and quantity.
5. The page validates quantity and that a rental duration has been selected.
6. The user clicks the "Add to Cart" action for the rental configuration.
7. The page calls `addItemToCart` with `itemType = 'rental'`, passing `productId`, `quantity`, and `rentalDuration`.
8. On success, the cart summary is updated from the returned `cart` object, and the returned `message` is displayed.

Review cart status from product page:
1. On page load (after `getProductDetails`), the page calls `getCartSummary` to retrieve the current cart snapshot.
2. The cart summary widget displays the total amount, discount, item count, applied promo (if any), and shows the `minimum_order_amount` and `currency` from `getCartSummary`.
3. After each successful `addItemToCart` call, the page uses the `cart` object from the response to update the cart summary immediately, without requiring another `getCartSummary` call.
4. The user can click the cart icon or a "View Cart" button to navigate to `cart.html`.

Handle navigation and error states:
1. If the `id` parameter is missing or empty on page initialization, the page shows an error state indicating that the product cannot be found and hides purchase interactions.
2. If `getProductDetails` fails or returns an error, the page shows an error message and does not allow adding to cart.
3. If `addItemToCart` returns `success = false`, the page displays the returned `message` and keeps the current configuration so the user can adjust it.
4. The user can click the breadcrumb link to return to the product listing page, which navigates to `products.html` with a `categoryId` query parameter derived from the loaded product’s `category_id`.
5. The user can click the logo in the header to navigate back to `index.html`.
interactions: On page initialization: read the `id` URL parameter. If present, call `getProductDetails(id)` and, independently, call `getCartSummary()` to initialize the cart summary widget.

Mode selection interaction: user clicks a tab or radio button for one-time, subscription, or rental. The page updates the active configuration mode state and shows or hides configuration controls based on `purchase_options.can_one_time`, `purchase_options.can_subscription`, and `purchase_options.can_rental`.

Quantity input interaction: user changes the quantity via a numeric input or stepper control. The configuration state is updated and validated in real time; invalid quantities disable the "Add to Cart" submission.

Subscription configuration interaction: when the subscription mode is active, user selects values from dropdowns bound to `purchase_options.subscription_frequency_options` and `purchase_options.delivery_day_options`. The chosen option `value` fields are stored in the configuration state as `subscriptionFrequency` and `subscriptionDeliveryDayOfWeek`.

Rental configuration interaction: when the rental mode is active, user selects a rental duration from a dropdown bound to `purchase_options.rental_duration_options`, storing the selected `value` as `rentalDuration` in the configuration state.

"Add to Cart" submission for the current mode: user submits the add-to-cart form. The page validates the configuration based on the current mode. If valid, it calls `addItemToCart` with the appropriate combination of parameters drawn from the active configuration and the current `productId`.
Cart summary interaction: cart summary widget in the header or sidebar reacts to updated cart data. It can be clicked to navigate to `cart.html`.
Breadcrumb interaction: user clicks the category breadcrumb. The page navigates to `products.html` with `categoryId` set to the current product’s `category_id`.
Header logo interaction: user clicks the site logo, and the browser navigates to `index.html`.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is determined by the `id` URL parameter (`product_detail.html?id={productId}`). On initial load and whenever the `id` parameter changes (such as via internal navigation or browser history navigation), the page reads the parameter, clears any previous product-specific state, and calls `getProductDetails` for the new `productId`. No product-specific configuration state (such as selected mode, quantity, or frequency) is persisted in URL parameters; it exists only in memory and resets when the `id` parameter changes or the page is reloaded. The listing breadcrumb URL is constructed as `products.html?categoryId={product.category_id}`, which uses the `category_id` of the loaded product. Navigation to the cart page (`cart.html`) and home page (`index.html`) does not carry additional parameters from this page. The cart state is not stored in URL parameters; instead, it is obtained from `getCartSummary` or the `cart` object returned by `addItemToCart`. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used on this page; URL parameters and API responses are the only state sources.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading the `id` URL parameter, requesting product details via `getProductDetails`, and exposing the loaded `product` and `purchase_options` to other components. Manages loading and error states for product data.
event_handlers: onInit: read `id` from the current URL query string. If `id` is present, call `getProductDetails(productId = id)`. If `id` is missing, set a 'product not found' error state.
onProductDetailsSuccess: store `product` and `purchase_options` in shared page state; clear error and loading flags.
onProductDetailsError: set an error state indicating that the product details could not be loaded; prevent purchase-related interactions until resolved.
onUrlChange:id: when the `id` query parameter changes via navigation, reset product-specific state and call `getProductDetails` again for the new `id`.

components.functionality: Displays key product information at the top of the page, including name, basic description snippet, water type label, size label, packaging category label, rating and rating count, and badges such as eco-friendly or BPA free. Does not include purchase controls.
event_handlers: onClick:badge: optionally open contextual information about a selected badge or tag (handled by a client-side modal or tooltip; no additional interface calls).

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the main product listing for the product’s category and to home, using the product’s `category_id` to build the listing URL.
event_handlers: onClick:category: navigate to `products.html?categoryId={product.category_id}` using the `category_id` from the loaded product.
onClick:currentProduct: no navigation; represents the current page.

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose between one-time purchase, subscription, and rental modes based on availability flags from `purchase_options` and the product. Controls which configuration panel (one-time, subscription, rental) is visible.
event_handlers: onInit: determine initial active mode using `purchase_options` availability flags and product flags (e.g., choose a mode that is allowed for the product). Store the selected mode in shared page state as `currentItemType`.
onChange:mode: when user selects a different mode, update `currentItemType` to one of `one_time`, `subscription`, or `rental` and notify configuration panels to show/hide accordingly.

components.functionality: Offers a numeric input or stepper for the user to set the quantity for the selected purchase mode. The control is shared across modes and initializes from `purchase_options.default_quantity`.
event_handlers: onInit: set initial quantity to `purchase_options.default_quantity` if provided; otherwise use a business-rule-defined minimum quantity.
onChange:quantity: update shared configuration state `quantity` and perform client-side validation (e.g., ensure quantity is greater than zero).
onValidationFailure: mark the input as invalid, disable submission in `add-to-cart-form`, and display a validation message.
onValidationSuccess: clear validation errors and enable submission when all other required fields are valid.

components.functionality: Shown only when the subscription mode is active. Lets the user choose subscription frequency and delivery day of week from options returned by `getProductDetails`, in addition to using the global quantity from `quantity-selector`.
event_handlers: onModeChange: when `currentItemType` becomes `subscription`, show this panel; when mode changes away, hide it and optionally reset its internal selections.
onChange:subscriptionFrequency: when the user selects an option, store its `value` in shared configuration state as `subscriptionFrequency`.
onChange:deliveryDay: when the user selects an option, store its `value` in shared configuration state as `subscriptionDeliveryDayOfWeek`.
onValidate: ensure that a frequency and delivery day are both selected before allowing the subscription configuration to be submitted.

components.functionality: Shown only when the rental mode is active. Lets the user choose a rental duration from options returned by `getProductDetails`, in addition to using the global quantity from `quantity-selector`.
event_handlers: onModeChange: when `currentItemType` becomes `rental`, show this panel; when mode changes away, hide it and optionally reset its internal selections.
onChange:rentalDuration: when the user selects a rental duration option, store its `value` in shared configuration state as `rentalDuration`.
onValidate: ensure that a rental duration is selected before allowing the rental configuration to be submitted.

components.functionality: Shown only when the one-time purchase mode is active. It primarily reuses the shared quantity selector and can present per-unit price information based on `product.price` and `product.currency`.
event_handlers: onModeChange: when `currentItemType` becomes `one_time`, show this panel; when mode changes away, hide it.

components.functionality: Aggregates the selected mode, quantity, and any mode-specific configuration (subscription or rental) into a single submission that calls `addItemToCart`. Handles validation, success, and error feedback, and triggers cart summary updates on success.
event_handlers: onSubmit: before calling `addItemToCart`, validate that quantity is valid and required configuration fields for the current mode are present. If validation fails, prevent submission.

onSubmitSuccess: when `addItemToCart` returns with `success = true`, update the cart summary component with the returned `cart`, display the success `message`, and keep the configuration state in case the user wants to add more.
onSubmitError: when `addItemToCart` returns `success = false` or errors, display the returned `message` as an error and do not clear the current configuration, so the user can correct it.

onCallAddItemToCart: call `addItemToCart` with parameters:
- `productId = product.id`
- `itemType = currentItemType`
- `quantity = quantity`
- `subscriptionFrequency = subscriptionFrequency` only if `itemType` is `subscription`
- `subscriptionDeliveryDayOfWeek = subscriptionDeliveryDayOfWeek` only if `itemType` is `subscription`
- `rentalDuration = rentalDuration` only if `itemType` is `rental`.
onCartUpdate: after a successful `addItemToCart` response, propagate the `cart` object to `cart-summary-widget` for display.
