component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily static, but may optionally adopt a subtle sticky behavior on taller forms: on desktop, once the user scrolls past the top of the actions bar, it can stick to the bottom edge of the viewport with a slight shadow, ensuring that the primary CTA remains visible without overwhelming the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription status indicator is visually integrated into the top-right area of the subscription summary panel. It appears as a compact badge or pill—using the bright aqua or orange brand color depending on status (e.g., aqua for active, muted grey for paused, orange for attention)—aligned flush with the panel’s right edge and top-aligned with the primary subscription title on the left. It occupies roughly 1–2 columns in width and sits with 8–12px of padding inside, making it clearly legible without dominating the entire panel. There is 8–16px of whitespace around the indicator to separate it from nearby text (such as subscription ID or status description) so it reads as a distinct visual token. Its color contrast makes it a secondary focal point after the subscription name, guiding the user’s eye from left to right across the summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position relative to the contents of the subscription summary panel. It scrolls together with the panel and does not stick independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback notification banner is a full-width bar positioned directly below the global header and above the subscription summary panel. It spans the entire viewport width and uses a slightly elevated background color—either a pale aqua or a soft orange tint—depending on the notification type, with a subtle bottom border or shadow to separate it from the content below. Inside, content is constrained to the same centered max-width grid, with the message text aligned to the left and any dismiss/close icon or inline link aligned to the right. Vertically, the banner is shallow but readable, typically 48–64px high, with 12–16px padding top and bottom and 16–24px horizontal padding on each side of its inner content. When present, it pushes the rest of the page content downward rather than overlapping it, preserving the orderly, linear flow seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow when visible, occupying its own band below the header. It may slide in/out on appearance but does not overlay or float above other content and is dismissible to reclaim vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error gate wraps the entire main content area beneath the header and feedback banner, controlling whether the user sees the normal subscription summary + settings layout, the centered loader, or an error state. Spatially, it reserves the same central column and approximate height as the combined summary panel and settings form so the overall layout doesn’t jump dramatically when content loads. In an error state, it displays a centered error message panel within this region, aligned much like the loader: centered horizontally within the grid and positioned around the vertical center of the available viewport space. This error panel might occupy roughly 40–60% of the main content width, with generous padding (24–32px) and whitespace around it to retain the clean, professional feel. When content loads successfully, the gate simply reveals the subscription-summary-panel at the top of its region, followed by the subscription-settings-form and subscription-form-actions, maintaining the standard vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a conditional container governing what is rendered in the main content region. It does not introduce its own fixed or sticky behavior but determines whether the loader, error content, or normal page components are visible.

name: Addresses
title: Addresses
description: Manage saved delivery addresses used for checkout, subscriptions, and deliveries, including adding, editing, deleting, and setting a default address.

core_features: Fetch and display the user’s saved addresses, including which one is currently marked as default, using getAddresses.
Provide an address creation flow where the user can enter label, street, city, state, ZIP code, optionally set it as default, and submit via createAddress.
Provide an address editing flow where an existing address is loaded into a form, editable, and saved via updateAddress.
Allow deletion of an address with user confirmation, call deleteAddress, and update the list based on the returned remaining_addresses and message.
Allow marking an address as the default by updating it via updateAddress with setAsDefault, ensuring only one address is default according to API behavior.
Support navigation back to account.html via breadcrumb or a link, and navigation to upcoming_deliveries.html after a successful add or edit via a dedicated call-to-action.
Reflect page mode (list, add, edit) and current selection via URL parameters, without using localStorage, so the page can be deep-linked and is restorable on refresh.
user_workflows: View all addresses (list mode): 1) User opens addresses.html without specific mode parameters. 2) On initialisation, the page reads URL parameters (e.g., mode, addressId) and determines that it should be in list mode. 3) The page calls getAddresses with no parameters. 4) When getAddresses returns, the page populates the address list with each address record and highlights or indicates the default address based on is_default and/or default_address_id. 5) The user can from here choose to add a new address, edit an existing one, or delete an address using the actions provided on each address row.

Add a new address: 1) From list mode, user clicks an “Add address” action. 2) The page updates the URL to addresses.html?mode=add and switches to add mode (optionally keeping the list visible or focusing solely on the form). 3) An empty address form is shown with fields for label, street, city, state, zipCode, and a checkbox or toggle for setAsDefault. 4) User fills in the fields and submits the form. 5) The page validates input on the client side (e.g., required fields not empty) and, if valid, calls createAddress with the entered values and setAsDefault flag. 6) On success: the page displays a success notification using the returned message, updates its in-memory address list either by appending the returned address or by re-calling getAddresses, updates the URL back to list mode (e.g., addresses.html?mode=list&highlightAddressId={newId}), and reveals a “View upcoming deliveries” call-to-action that navigates to upcoming_deliveries.html when clicked. 7) On failure: the page shows the message from the API and keeps the form open for correction.

Edit an existing address: 1) In list mode, each address row provides an “Edit” action. 2) When the user clicks Edit for a specific address, the page updates the URL to addresses.html?mode=edit&addressId={selectedId}. 3) The page resolves the address data for addressId from the already-loaded addresses; if they are not available (e.g., direct deep link), it calls getAddresses, then finds the matching address. 4) The address form is populated with the existing values (label, street, city, state, zip_code, and default status). 5) User modifies the fields and submits the form. 6) The page calls updateAddress with addressId and the updated field values plus setAsDefault flag. 7) On success: the page shows the success message from the API, updates the address in the in-memory list or re-calls getAddresses, updates the URL to list mode (e.g., addresses.html?mode=list&highlightAddressId={addressId}), and shows the “View upcoming deliveries” call-to-action linking to upcoming_deliveries.html. 8) On failure: the page shows the message from the API and keeps the form in edit mode to allow further changes.

Set an address as default: 1) In list mode or edit mode, the user triggers an action to mark a specific address as default (either by toggling a control in the list row or by checking setAsDefault in the form). 2) If done from the list, the page opens the edit form for that address (addresses.html?mode=edit&addressId={id}) with the default control pre-selected based on the current is_default. 3) User confirms or adjusts the default setting and submits. 4) The page calls updateAddress with setAsDefault set according to the user choice. 5) On success: the UI updates the list to reflect which address is default, ensuring the default indicator matches the API’s updated state (e.g., only the address with is_default true is marked).

Delete an address: 1) In list mode, each address row provides a Delete action. 2) User clicks Delete for a specific address. 3) The page opens a confirmation dialog that explains that deletion will remove the address from future use, and asks the user to confirm or cancel. 4) If the user cancels, the dialog closes and no API call is made. 5) If the user confirms, the page calls deleteAddress with addressId for the selected address. 6) On success (success=true): the page updates the in-memory list using remaining_addresses from the response and re-renders the list. If the deleted address was default, the default indicator is updated according to the is_default flags on remaining_addresses. 7) On failure (success=false): the page shows the returned message and keeps the address in the list. If the failure is due to dependencies with active subscriptions or upcoming deliveries, this information is conveyed entirely by the API message; the page just displays it.

Navigate back to My Account: 1) The page header or breadcrumb includes an element linking back to account.html. 2) When the user clicks this link, the browser navigates to account.html without calling any interfaces from this page. 3) If the user arrived with URL parameters (e.g., from=checkout), those parameters can optionally be forwarded in the query string according to navigation design rules, but this page itself does not depend on them.

Navigate to upcoming deliveries after address changes: 1) After a successful createAddress or updateAddress call, the page shows a dedicated “View upcoming deliveries” link or button. 2) When the user clicks this element, the browser navigates to upcoming_deliveries.html with no required parameters (or with optional parameters if defined by that page’s contract, which are not relied on here). 3) The visibility or emphasis of this link can be controlled by page state (e.g., a flag set after last successful save) and is not dependent on localStorage.
interactions: Page load and initial data fetch: On document ready or equivalent lifecycle event, the page reads URL parameters such as mode, addressId, and highlightAddressId. If mode is not present, it defaults to list mode. The page then calls getAddresses to retrieve the current list of addresses and default_address_id. On response, it stores the addresses in internal state and renders components dependent on this data.

Add address button click: In list mode, the page shows an interactive element to start adding a new address. When this element is clicked, the page updates the URL to include mode=add (e.g., addresses.html?mode=add) using history.pushState or a full reload, and shows the address form in add mode with empty fields and default checkbox unselected or set according to design without using API data.

Address form input: As the user types into the label, street, city, state, and ZIP fields, the form’s local state is updated. Input validation runs on blur or form submit, ensuring required fields are provided. No interface is called until the user submits.

Address form submit (add mode): When the user submits in add mode, the form gathers label, street, city, state, zipCode, and setAsDefault from current form state, and calls createAddress with those parameters. While waiting for the response, the form shows a pending state (e.g., disabled submit) and avoids duplicate submissions. After receiving the response: if success is true, it updates list state, resets the form or returns to list, updates URL parameters, raises a success notification, and sets a flag indicating a recent successful save so that the “View upcoming deliveries” CTA is visible. If success is false, it displays the message and keeps the form values.

Address form submit (edit mode): When the user submits in edit mode, the form gathers the same fields plus the current addressId (from URL params or internal selection) and calls updateAddress. While waiting, it blocks duplicate submissions. After receiving the response: if success is true, it updates the corresponding address in the list or re-fetches addresses via getAddresses, updates URL to list mode with highlightAddressId set to addressId, shows a success notification, and enables the “View upcoming deliveries” CTA. If success is false, it shows the message and stays in edit mode.

Edit link click in address list: Each address row has an Edit control. When clicked, the page sets mode=edit and addressId to the clicked address’s id in the URL, and then opens the address form populated from the current list state. If for any reason the address is not present in memory (e.g., direct deep link), the page ensures getAddresses has been called and then finds the address before displaying the form.

Delete link click in address list: Each address row has a Delete control. When clicked, the page opens a confirmation dialog component with a message describing the action. The dialog exposes Confirm and Cancel actions. No API calls are made until Confirm is chosen.

Delete confirmation: When the user confirms deletion in the dialog, the page calls deleteAddress with addressId. Upon response: if success=true, the dialog closes, the list is replaced by remaining_addresses from the response, and a notification is shown using the message. If success=false, the dialog may close or remain open according to design, but in either case the page shows the message and keeps the address in the list.

Default toggle interaction: If default state is changeable from the list, clicking the default toggle for an address triggers navigation into edit mode for that address (mode=edit&addressId={id}) with the default control in the form pre-checked or toggled based on the click. Submitting the form then calls updateAddress with setAsDefault determined from the updated default control.

Breadcrumb or Back to My Account click: A navigation element, such as a breadcrumb item, links to account.html. Clicking this element performs a standard browser navigation to account.html without invoking any of the page’s interfaces.

View upcoming deliveries CTA click: After a successful address creation or update, the page displays an interactive element that navigates to upcoming_deliveries.html. Clicking this element does not call any interface on this page; it solely changes the location.

URL parameter changes via navigation: If the user navigates directly to addresses.html with parameters (e.g., addresses.html?mode=edit&addressId={id}), the page reads these parameters on load, determines that edit mode is required, calls getAddresses, and once data is available, auto-opens the edit form for the specified address. Similarly, if highlightAddressId is present, the list uses that parameter to emphasize the corresponding address row once the data is loaded.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page relies on URL parameters instead of localStorage to control its primary state. The base URL is addresses.html. The key parameters are: 1) mode: controls the high-level view state; expected values include 'list', 'add', and 'edit'. If mode is missing or unsupported, the page falls back to 'list'. - mode=list: the address list is shown, and the address form is hidden unless the UI design supports showing both. - mode=add: the list may be shown or hidden according to design, but the address form appears in add mode with empty values ready for createAddress. - mode=edit: the list may be shown or hidden, and the address form appears in edit mode populated with the address specified by addressId. 2) addressId: used when mode=edit to identify which address should be loaded into the form. On page init, once getAddresses resolves, the page searches the returned addresses for a match and binds that record to the form. If not found, the page can show an error state or fall back to list mode without making any additional API calls. addressId is not used when mode is 'list' or 'add'. 3) highlightAddressId: used after successful createAddress or updateAddress to visually emphasize the modified or newly created address in the list. After a save, the page can navigate to addresses.html?mode=list&highlightAddressId={id}. When rendering the list, the component compares each address.id to highlightAddressId and applies a transient highlight treatment. Highlighting is purely presentational and does not require any additional API calls. 4) afterSave: an optional flag that can be set (e.g., addresses.html?mode=list&afterSave=1&highlightAddressId={id}) to indicate that the page was just returned from a successful save operation. If this flag is present, the page shows a success notification and the 'View upcoming deliveries' CTA. The flag can be cleared by updating the URL after the initial render, ensuring that reloading the page does not repeatedly show the post-save state. 5) other passthrough parameters: parameters not recognized by the Addresses page are ignored for state decisions but preserved when generating navigation links if required by global navigation conventions. Internal state, such as the current form field values and whether a network call is in flight, is held in runtime memory only and is not persisted in URL parameters or localStorage. getAddresses is always invoked on initial page load, and optionally after createAddress, updateAddress, or deleteAddress if the implementation chooses to refresh from the source of truth instead of mutating local state from the responses.

components.functionality: Central controller for the Addresses page that reads URL parameters, orchestrates calls to getAddresses, tracks the current mode (list/add/edit), holds the in-memory list of addresses, and coordinates when child components should render or refresh.
event_handlers: onChangeMode(newMode, optionalAddressId) -> updates URL and internal mode state
onAddressCreated(address) -> updates internal list or triggers getAddresses, updates URL with highlightAddressId, sets afterSave flag
onAddressUpdated(address) -> updates internal list or triggers getAddresses, updates URL with highlightAddressId, sets afterSave flag
onShowNotificationFromApi(message) -> forwards notification text to notification component
onClearAfterSaveFlag() -> updates URL to remove afterSave, if present

components.functionality: Displays navigational elements for the page, including a link or breadcrumb item that returns the user to account.html.

Shows the list of saved addresses returned by getAddresses, indicating each address’s label and full_address, and visually identifying the default address. Provides per-row actions for editing and deleting, and optionally for initiating default changes via edit mode.
event_handlers: onClickAddAddressButton() -> notifies page-controller to change mode to 'add' and updates URL accordingly
onClickEditAddress(addressId) -> notifies page-controller to change mode to 'edit' with addressId and updates URL
onClickDeleteAddress(addressId) -> opens delete-confirmation-dialog with selected addressId
onRenderHighlightRow(highlightAddressId) -> uses URL parameter to apply highlight to matching address row

components.functionality: Handles both creation and editing of an address. In add mode, it presents empty fields and on submit calls createAddress. In edit mode, it pre-populates fields from the selected Address and on submit calls updateAddress. It includes a control for setAsDefault so the user can designate the address as default.
event_handlers: onInitFromModeAndAddress(mode, addressId, addressesList) -> loads appropriate initial form values
onChangeField(fieldName, value) -> updates local form state
onSubmitAddForm(label, street, city, state, zipCode, setAsDefault) -> calls createAddress and passes response to page-controller
onSubmitEditForm(addressId, label, street, city, state, zipCode, setAsDefault) -> calls updateAddress and passes response to page-controller
onCancelForm() -> notifies page-controller to switch back to list mode and update URL (mode=list)

components.functionality: Presents a confirmation step before deleting an address. When opened with a specific addressId, it asks the user to confirm or cancel. On confirmation, it calls deleteAddress and relays the result back to the page controller and notification system.
event_handlers: onOpen(addressId) -> stores addressId to be deleted
onClickCancel() -> closes dialog without calling deleteAddress
onClickConfirm(addressId) -> calls deleteAddress, then on success or failure returns success flag, message, and remaining_addresses (if any) to page-controller
onDeleteSuccess(remaining_addresses, message) -> closes dialog, notifies page-controller to replace list with remaining_addresses, and triggers notification with message
onDeleteFailure(message) -> closes or keeps dialog according to design, and triggers notification with message

components.functionality: Displays feedback messages from createAddress, updateAddress, deleteAddress, or other page events, such as success or error states. Messages are populated solely from API responses or from explicit page-state transitions, not from hardcoded data payloads.
event_handlers: onShow(message, severity) -> displays a notification with given message and severity level (e.g., success, error)
onApiResponseMessage(message) -> triggered by page-controller when an API call returns a message field

components.functionality: Shown when the page is in a post-save state (e.g., after a successful createAddress or updateAddress) or when the afterSave URL flag is present. Provides navigation to upcoming_deliveries.html so the user can verify or manage deliveries associated with their updated addresses.
event_handlers: onDetermineVisibility(afterSaveFlag, lastSaveSuccess) -> decides whether to render the CTA based on page-controller state or URL parameters

components.functionality: Abstracts reading and writing of URL parameters to ensure the page state (mode, addressId, highlightAddressId, afterSave) is in sync with the browser URL. It is used by the page controller and other components when they need to change mode or highlight an address.
event_handlers: onReadUrlParams() -> returns current values for mode, addressId, highlightAddressId, afterSave
onUpdateUrlParams(params) -> updates the query string while preserving unrelated parameters as needed
onPopState() -> listens to browser history changes and informs page-controller of updated URL state so the page can re-render accordingly

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom hierarchy as the mockup: global site header and hero (shared across the site) remain at the top, and the Addresses page content begins below the hero as a full-width white band. Inside this band, components are arranged in a structured linear flow: first a breadcrumb navigation aligned to the top-left of the content area, then (when present) a notification banner spanning the content width, and below that a three-column layout that mirrors the mockup’s order/contact band. In this three-column area, the left column is dedicated to the address list, the middle column to the address form, and the right column to contextual information and navigation in the form of the upcoming-deliveries CTA. The delete-confirmation dialog appears as an overlay centered on the viewport, independent of the main flow, while the page controller and URL state sync sit behind the scenes orchestrating behavior without visible UI.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom hierarchy as the mockup: global site header and hero (shared across the site) remain at the top, and the Addresses page content begins below the hero as a full-width white band. Inside this band, components are arranged in a structured linear flow: first a breadcrumb navigation aligned to the top-left of the content area, then (when present) a notification banner spanning the content width, and below that a three-column layout that mirrors the mockup’s order/contact band. In this three-column area, the left column is dedicated to the address list, the middle column to the address form, and the right column to contextual information and navigation in the form of the upcoming-deliveries CTA. The delete-confirmation dialog appears as an overlay centered on the viewport, independent of the main flow, while the page controller and URL state sync sit behind the scenes orchestrating behavior without visible UI.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the global header and hero, the main Addresses content band occupies roughly the remaining 45–55% of the initial viewport height, with the capacity to scroll vertically as needed. Horizontally, the content is constrained to a centered max-width container (e.g., ~1120–1200px), using an implied 12-column grid. The three main columns (address list, address form, upcoming-deliveries CTA) each span approximately 4 grid columns (about one third of the content width), separated by generous gutters of about 24–32px. The breadcrumb and notification banner span most or all of the content width but remain visually shallow to preserve emphasis on the three-column section beneath. The delete-confirmation dialog takes up about 30–40% of the viewport width and is vertically centered, reserving ample whitespace around it. Vertical section spacing follows the mockup: 32–48px between the hero and the top of the Addresses band, and 24–32px between the breadcrumb, notification banner, and the three-column area.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout maintains the mockup’s moderately spacious density. Each vertical stack (breadcrumb row, notification banner, three-column section) is clearly separated using 24–32px of vertical spacing. Within the address list and form, items and fields are separated by 16–24px of vertical space, keeping the interface easy to scan even when many addresses or fields are present. Inputs follow the observed padding pattern: roughly 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal internal padding. The right-hand upcoming-deliveries CTA is treated as a light, airy informational card with comfortable padding and whitespace around its elements, reinforcing hierarchy without crowding the main functional columns. The modal dialog is compact but not cramped, with 24–32px internal padding and clear separation between its body text and action buttons.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual journey mirrors the mockup’s Z-pattern and top-down flow. The user’s eye first engages with the global header and hero (logo, navigation, and primary site-level CTA), then drops into the Addresses page area. Within the Addresses band, the eye starts at the top-left breadcrumb (indicating context and section), moves horizontally across to notice any notification banner messaging, and then descends into the three-column block. There, the eye naturally lands on the left column (address list of existing saved addresses), moves across to the middle column (the active address form for adding or editing), and finally reaches the right column (upcoming deliveries CTA and related contextual help). When a delete action is triggered, the visual flow is temporarily dominated by the centered modal dialog, which sits above a dimmed backdrop, making the confirm/cancel actions the sole focus until dismissed.

layout.overall_description: Beneath the site’s full-width header and photographic hero, the Addresses page appears as a clean, centered white band that stretches horizontally across most of the viewport, anchored to a 12-column grid. At the very top-left of this band, a subtle breadcrumb trail shows the user’s path (e.g., Home › Account › Addresses), with enough breathing room above and below to feel deliberate rather than cramped. If there are any success or error messages, a colored notification banner sits directly below the breadcrumb, spanning nearly the full content width in a shallow strip, using the brand’s aqua or orange accents to stand out without overwhelming the page. Below this header row of meta-information, the main Addresses area divides into three equal-width vertical columns, each separated by generous gutters: on the left, a vertical stack of address cards showing saved delivery locations; in the center, a tidy, stacked address form used for adding or editing; and on the right, a shorter informational card prompting the user to view or adjust upcoming deliveries. All three columns start at the same vertical baseline, echoing the alignment of form fields in the original order/contact band. Inputs and cards are padded generously and spaced 16–24px apart, giving the section a calm, corporate feel with clear scan lines. When the user initiates a delete, the rest of the page subtly fades under a semi-transparent overlay, and a compact confirmation dialog appears in the middle of the screen, framed by ample whitespace and clearly separated primary and secondary actions. Throughout, the layout preserves the mockup’s sense of structured simplicity: a strong horizontal top, a visually dominant central band with columns, and a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom progression of attention.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The addresses-page-controller acts as the main container for the Addresses section, starting just below the global hero image and spanning the full browser width. Inside, it centers a max-width content area (approximately 1120–1200px) using equal left and right margins, aligned to the site’s 12-column grid. Vertically, it introduces a top padding of about 32–40px from the bottom edge of the hero to the first in-page element (the breadcrumb-navigation), and about 40–48px of bottom padding below the lowest content elements to create a soft end to the page. Within its boundaries, elements are stacked in a clear vertical order: breadcrumb-navigation at the top, notification-banner immediately beneath when present, and then a three-column grid row that houses the address-list (left third), address-form (middle third), and upcoming-deliveries-cta (right third). The delete-confirmation-dialog is rendered by this controller but floats above it when active, and the url-state-sync lives here without adding any visible structure. The controller ensures consistent horizontal gutters (approximately 24–32px) between the three columns and maintains the base 8px spacing rhythm across all nested components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level container that scrolls with the page, defining the central content column and the three-column grid inside it without itself rendering visible chrome.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb-navigation is positioned at the top-left of the Addresses content band inside the addresses-page-controller, directly under the bottom edge of the hero section. It aligns with the left edge of the main content grid (column 1 of 12) and typically spans across the left 6–8 columns, leaving some empty space to the right for openness rather than filling the entire width. Vertically, it occupies a shallow strip—about 24–32px high—with 12–16px of vertical padding above and below its text, following the 8px spacing system. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the breadcrumb and the element below it (either the notification-banner or, if no banner is present, the top of the three-column layout), preserving a clear separation between navigation context and main functional area. The breadcrumb text is left-aligned and sits flush with the content column edges, creating a strong starting anchor for the user’s reading flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls with the page; it remains at the top of the Addresses section and does not stick to the viewport when scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner appears immediately below the breadcrumb-navigation within the same main content width. It is a full-width bar relative to the content container, spanning from the left grid edge (column 1) to the right grid edge (column 12), with subtle rounded corners if used elsewhere in the system. The banner has a height dictated by its message content, but typically it forms a strip around 48–64px tall, with internal padding of about 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. Its background uses a tinted aqua or orange to stand out against the white page, while still fitting into the corporate palette. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the banner and the top of the three-column address section, so the band reads as its own layer of information. When absent, the three-column layout shifts up, occupying the space where the banner would have been, maintaining consistent top padding defined by the addresses-page-controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element that pushes surrounding content up or down when shown; it does not overlay other elements and scrolls away with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The address-list occupies the entire left third of the main three-column layout, corresponding to approximately columns 1–4 of the 12-column grid inside the addresses-page-controller. Its top aligns horizontally with the top of the address-form and upcoming-deliveries-cta, forming a clean row of column headers or first elements. The list area runs vertically from this baseline down toward the bottom padding of the page, taking up as much height as its content requires. At the top of this column, a section title such as “Saved addresses” appears, with about 8–12px of space beneath it before the first address card. Each address in the list is represented as a full-width card within this column, with 16–24px of internal padding and 16–24px of vertical spacing between cards, maintaining the moderate content density. If the number of addresses grows beyond what fits comfortably in the viewport, the column can scroll with the page as usual; on taller screens, the user will see more cards at once. The left edge of the address-list is the primary visual anchor for the main content area, echoing the left-most input column in the mockup’s form band and naturally drawing the eye first within this section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static column content that scrolls with the page; it shares equal column width with the address-form and upcoming-deliveries-cta and respects consistent gutters between columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The address-form is placed in the center column of the three-column layout, occupying approximately columns 5–8 of the 12-column grid—roughly one third of the content width with 24–32px gutters separating it from the address-list on the left and the upcoming-deliveries-cta on the right. Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the address-list, creating a uniform row of column headers. Within this column, the form fields are stacked vertically with clear, consistent spacing: each field (or horizontal pair of fields, such as city and ZIP) is separated by about 16–24px of vertical space, while fields themselves have 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal internal padding. Key actions, such as “Save address” and “Cancel,” are placed at the bottom of the form, right-aligned within the column, with 16–24px of space above them to separate from the last input. The form column has top and bottom padding of around 8–16px to prevent fields from hugging the column boundaries. This central placement and the alignment with the address-list visually express that the form is the primary interactive area for modifying the selected address, mirroring the middle-column fields in the original mockup’s order/contact band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static middle column content that scrolls with the page, maintaining equal width with the other columns and aligning to the grid’s gutters for a balanced three-column structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delete-confirmation-dialog appears as a modal overlay centered in the viewport whenever a delete action is triggered from the address-list or address-form. It sits above a semi-transparent dark backdrop that covers the entire viewport, subtly dimming the underlying page while still allowing the three-column layout to be vaguely visible. The dialog itself is a rectangular card with a width of about 30–40% of the viewport (with a minimum around 320px and a maximum around 520–560px), positioned both horizontally and vertically centered. It has generous internal padding—approximately 24–32px on all sides—keeping the confirmation message, warning icon (if any), and action buttons well spaced. The message text is placed near the top, followed by about 16–24px of vertical space before the action buttons. The primary and secondary buttons are right-aligned at the bottom of the dialog, with 8–16px of space between them. The dialog does not extend to page edges; instead, it sits in a comfortable bubble of whitespace, reinforcing focus and matching the mockup’s corporate, uncluttered aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport with a full-screen dimmed backdrop; it remains in place while the underlying page is scroll-locked until the dialog is dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner appears immediately below the breadcrumb-navigation within the same main content width. It is a full-width bar relative to the content container, spanning from the left grid edge (column 1) to the right grid edge (column 12), with subtle rounded corners if used elsewhere in the system. The banner has a height dictated by its message content, but typically it forms a strip around 48–64px tall, with internal padding of about 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. Its background uses a tinted aqua or orange to stand out against the white page, while still fitting into the corporate palette. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space between the bottom of the banner and the top of the three-column address section, so the band reads as its own layer of information. When absent, the three-column layout shifts up, occupying the space where the banner would have been, maintaining consistent top padding defined by the addresses-page-controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline element that pushes surrounding content up or down when shown; it does not overlay other elements and scrolls away with the rest of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming-deliveries-cta occupies the right-most third of the three-column layout, aligned with approximately columns 9–12 of the 12-column grid. Its top baseline matches that of the address-list and address-form, creating a unified horizontal row. Within this right column, the CTA is presented as a card-like block rather than filling the entire column height. The card begins with a concise heading (e.g., “Upcoming deliveries”), followed by a short explanatory sentence and a prominent button (e.g., “View deliveries”) stacked vertically. Internally, the card uses around 16–24px padding on all sides, with 8–16px vertical spacing between the heading, body text, and button. The button is left-aligned within the card to mirror the left alignment of content in other columns while still clearly functioning as a CTA. Additional helpful text or a secondary link can sit below the primary button with lighter visual weight. The space below the card remains largely empty, reinforcing a spacious feel and ensuring the main focus stays on the left and middle functional columns, while still offering an easy path to manage how addresses affect upcoming deliveries.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static right-hand column content that scrolls with the page; behaves like an informational card within its column, not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component has no direct visual representation on the page; it is effectively invisible to the user. Spatially, it resides within the addresses-page-controller’s logical structure but occupies no width or height in the layout. Its influence is behavioral: it keeps the selected address, form mode (add vs. edit), and possibly modal state in sync with the URL so that deep links and browser navigation work intuitively. For instance, when a user selects an address from the address-list, the URL updates, and when the page is reloaded with that URL, the corresponding card in the address-list is highlighted and its details are loaded into the address-form. Despite this, there is no visible placeholder or empty area for it in the UI.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint behavior component; it does not affect spacing or positioning of any visible elements.

name: Upcoming Deliveries
title: Upcoming Deliveries
description: View all upcoming water deliveries in chronological order and reschedule individual deliveries by changing the address, date, and time window.

core_features: Fetch and display a chronological list of upcoming deliveries using getUpcomingDeliveries, including date, time window, delivery method, address, source, items summary, and status.
Highlight and auto-expand the reschedule interface for a specific delivery when the deliveryId URL parameter is present.
Open a reschedule interface for any listed delivery, loading its reschedulable options (addresses, date limits, available time windows) via getDeliveryRescheduleOptions.
Allow the user to modify the delivery address, date (via a constrained date picker), and time window and submit changes via rescheduleDelivery.
Update the on-page delivery list with the latest delivery details after a successful reschedule and show a confirmation or error message.
Provide navigation back to the My Account overview (account.html) and to the address management page (addresses.html) from within the reschedule flow.
user_workflows:
View all upcoming deliveries:
1. User navigates to upcoming_deliveries.html (optionally with a deliveryId parameter).
2. On page load, the page calls getUpcomingDeliveries with no parameters.
3. The page displays all returned deliveries sorted chronologically using their scheduled_date and time_window.
4. Each delivery row shows the scheduled date, time_window_label, delivery_method, address_label and address_full, items_summary, status, and source_label.
5. For deliveries that are still changeable based on business rules (as determined by backend-visible attributes like status), a Reschedule action is shown.

Deep-link to reschedule a specific delivery:
1. User arrives at upcoming_deliveries.html?deliveryId={id} from account.html or a direct link.
2. The page reads the deliveryId URL parameter and stores it as the focused delivery.
3. The page calls getUpcomingDeliveries and renders the list.
4. After rendering, the page locates the delivery matching the deliveryId parameter.
5. If found, the page automatically opens the reschedule interface for that delivery and calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(deliveryId).
6. The reschedule form is pre-populated with the delivery’s current address, date, and time window and shows all available rescheduling options.

Reschedule an upcoming delivery from the list:
1. User scrolls the list of upcoming deliveries.
2. User clicks the Reschedule action for a specific delivery.
3. The page updates the deliveryId URL parameter to the selected delivery’s ID (adding or replacing it).
4. The page calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions with that deliveryId.
5. When the options load, the reschedule form displays with:
   - The current delivery’s summary (date, time_window_label, address_full).
   - A dropdown of available_addresses.
   - A date picker constrained between earliest_available_date and earliest_available_date + max_schedule_days_ahead.
   - A dropdown of standard_delivery_time_windows, using the provided labels.
6. User selects a new address (if desired), picks a new date within the allowed range, and selects a new time window.
7. User submits the reschedule form.
8. The page calls rescheduleDelivery(deliveryId, newAddressId, newDate, newTimeWindow).
9. If success is true, the page:
   - Updates the corresponding delivery in the upcoming deliveries list with the returned delivery object.
   - Displays the success message from the API.
   - Optionally keeps the form open with updated values or collapses it; if collapsed, it may clear the deliveryId parameter.
10. If success is false, the page displays the message as an error and keeps the form open for corrections.

Manage addresses during rescheduling:
1. While filling the reschedule form, the user does not see the desired address in the available_addresses dropdown.
2. User clicks the Manage addresses link in the reschedule component.
3. The page navigates to addresses.html (no additional params required by this spec).
4. After managing addresses, the user can return to upcoming_deliveries.html (optionally including the same deliveryId to resume rescheduling).

Return to My Account overview:
1. User wants to leave the Upcoming Deliveries page.
2. User clicks the My Account breadcrumb link at the top of the page.
3. The page navigates to account.html without additional parameters (or as defined globally by the account page).
interactions: On page load, read the deliveryId URL parameter; call getUpcomingDeliveries; when data arrives, render the list and, if applicable, auto-open rescheduling for the matching delivery by calling getDeliveryRescheduleOptions.
Clicking the Reschedule action for a delivery sets or updates the deliveryId URL parameter and triggers getDeliveryRescheduleOptions for that specific delivery.
Changing the address selection in the reschedule form binds the selected available_addresses.id as newAddressId for submission.
Selecting a date in the reschedule date picker binds the chosen value as newDate, constrained using earliest_available_date and max_schedule_days_ahead from getDeliveryRescheduleOptions.
Selecting a time window in the reschedule form binds the chosen standard_delivery_time_windows.value as newTimeWindow.
Submitting the reschedule form triggers rescheduleDelivery with the currently bound deliveryId, newAddressId, newDate, and newTimeWindow.
On successful rescheduleDelivery response, update the corresponding delivery entry in the list with the returned delivery fields and display the returned message as a success notification.
On error or failed rescheduleDelivery response (success=false), display the returned message as an error notification and keep the form open.
Clicking a Close or Cancel action on the reschedule interface collapses it and clears the deliveryId URL parameter (or navigates to the same page without it).
Clicking the My Account breadcrumb navigates programmatically to account.html.
Clicking the Manage addresses link inside the reschedule form navigates to addresses.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses the deliveryId URL parameter to control which delivery, if any, has its reschedule interface open. On initial load, the page reads deliveryId from the query string. If no parameter is present, the page simply loads and renders the upcoming deliveries list via getUpcomingDeliveries with no reschedule interface open by default. If deliveryId is present, the page still calls getUpcomingDeliveries but, after rendering the list, it searches for the delivery whose delivery_id matches the parameter. If found, the page sets that delivery as the active reschedule target, visually expands its reschedule interface, and calls getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(deliveryId) to populate the form. When the user clicks Reschedule on a different delivery, the page updates the URL to upcoming_deliveries.html?deliveryId={newId} (replacing any existing value) using navigation or history APIs; the previous reschedule interface is collapsed, and getDeliveryRescheduleOptions is called for the newId. When the user closes the reschedule interface without saving, the page navigates to the same path without a deliveryId parameter (e.g., upcoming_deliveries.html) or updates the URL to remove deliveryId, which also clears the active reschedule target state. Optionally, after a successful reschedule, the page may either leave the deliveryId parameter in place (keeping the interface open and showing updated values) or remove it to return to a neutral list state; the chosen behavior must be consistent. No persistent state for this page is stored in localStorage or similar mechanisms; session state relies on in-memory component state plus URL parameters.

components.functionality: Displays the navigation path for the user and allows navigation back to the My Account overview page.
event_handlers: onClickMyAccount: navigate to account.html with no additional required parameters.
onClickCurrentPage: navigate or reload upcoming_deliveries.html, preserving or clearing URL parameters as appropriate.

components.functionality: Fetches the list of upcoming deliveries when the page loads and provides loading and error states to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: call getUpcomingDeliveries() with no parameters.
onReload: re-call getUpcomingDeliveries(), used after rescheduling or manual refresh.
onLoadSuccess: pass the returned deliveries array to upcoming-deliveries-list.
onLoadError: propagate an error state and message to notification-banner.

components.functionality: Displays the list of upcoming deliveries in chronological order and exposes per-delivery actions such as opening the reschedule interface.
event_handlers: onSort: sort deliveries by scheduled_date and time_window if client-side sorting is needed.
onClickReschedule(deliveryId): invoke url-state-manager to set URL deliveryId and trigger reschedule-options-loader for that delivery.
onDeliveryUpdated(updatedDelivery): replace the corresponding delivery item in the list after a successful reschedule.

components.functionality: Renders a single delivery entry, showing schedule details, items summary, and the Reschedule action if applicable.
event_handlers: onToggleDetails: expand or collapse additional delivery details if implemented.
onAutoExpandIfUrlMatches: when delivery_id matches the deliveryId URL parameter, automatically request opening the reschedule interface.

components.functionality: Manages reading and updating URL parameters relevant to this page, particularly deliveryId, and exposes the active deliveryId to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: read deliveryId from the current URL and expose it as activeDeliveryId.
setActiveDeliveryId(deliveryId): update the browser URL to include deliveryId as a query parameter and notify subscribers (such as reschedule-options-loader).
clearActiveDeliveryId: update the browser URL to remove the deliveryId parameter and notify subscribers.

components.functionality: Loads rescheduling options for the currently active delivery using getDeliveryRescheduleOptions and provides them to the reschedule form.
data_binding: Model:SiteSettings-like fields (earliest_available_date, max_schedule_days_ahead, standard_delivery_time_windows from getDeliveryRescheduleOptions response)
event_handlers: onActiveDeliveryIdChange: when url-state-manager sets a new deliveryId, call getDeliveryRescheduleOptions(deliveryId).
onLoadSuccess: provide delivery, available_addresses, earliest_available_date, max_schedule_days_ahead, and standard_delivery_time_windows to reschedule-form.
onLoadError: notify notification-banner and keep or close the reschedule UI depending on implementation.

components.functionality: Allows the user to change the address, date, and time window for the active delivery and submit the reschedule request.
event_handlers: onAddressChange(newAddressId): update in-form state for newAddressId based on the selected available_addresses.id.
onDateChange(newDate): update in-form state for newDate ensuring it falls between earliest_available_date and earliest_available_date plus max_schedule_days_ahead.
onTimeWindowChange(newTimeWindow): update in-form state for newTimeWindow from standard_delivery_time_windows.value.
onSubmit: call rescheduleDelivery(activeDeliveryId, newAddressId, newDate, newTimeWindow) and handle loading state.

onSubmitSuccess(response):
 - If response.success is true, emit an onDeliveryUpdated event with response.delivery to upcoming-deliveries-list.
 - Display response.message via notification-banner.
 - Optionally call url-state-manager.clearActiveDeliveryId to close the form.
onSubmitError(responseOrError): display an error message (either from response.message or a generic error) via notification-banner and keep the form open.
onCancel: collapse the reschedule interface for the active delivery and call url-state-manager.clearActiveDeliveryId.

components.functionality: Displays success, warning, or error messages for operations on the page, including loading failures and reschedule results.
event_handlers: onShowSuccess(message): render a transient or persistent success notification.
onShowError(message): render an error notification, typically triggered by failed getUpcomingDeliveries, getDeliveryRescheduleOptions, or rescheduleDelivery calls.
onDismiss: hide the current notification when the user closes it manually or after a timeout.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: The page adopts the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: (1) global header and hero remain consistent with the site; (2) directly below the hero, a full-width, shallow breadcrumbs bar sets the context; (3) immediately beneath, a full-width white content band acts as the main 'Upcoming Deliveries' area, divided into a multi-column layout using the 12-column grid. Within this band, the upcoming deliveries list occupies the larger left portion, and the reschedule form occupies a narrower right column, mirroring the original three-column order/contact band but simplified into a two-column functional layout. Notification messages appear just under the breadcrumbs at the top of this band, and loaders are positioned in-place over the areas they relate to.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page adopts the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: (1) global header and hero remain consistent with the site; (2) directly below the hero, a full-width, shallow breadcrumbs bar sets the context; (3) immediately beneath, a full-width white content band acts as the main 'Upcoming Deliveries' area, divided into a multi-column layout using the 12-column grid. Within this band, the upcoming deliveries list occupies the larger left portion, and the reschedule form occupies a narrower right column, mirroring the original three-column order/contact band but simplified into a two-column functional layout. Notification messages appear just under the breadcrumbs at the top of this band, and loaders are positioned in-place over the areas they relate to.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Following the mockup, the header and hero collectively occupy roughly the top half of the initial viewport. The breadcrumbs bar is a slim strip (about 8–10% of the viewport height for its area including margins). The main white content band fills the remainder of the first fold and extends downward as needed. Inside this band, the upcoming deliveries list takes roughly 2/3 of the usable content width (around 7–8 of 12 columns) and the reschedule form roughly 1/3 (around 4–5 columns), with generous gutters between them. Vertical spacing between sections (breadcrumbs to notification, notification to list/form band, list items spacing, form fields spacing) uses 16–32px increments, maintaining the airy feel. Loaders never add extra height; they replace or overlay their respective content spaces.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains moderate and spacious. The list of upcoming deliveries can contain many items, but each item is presented as a card-like row with clear padding and 16–24px vertical separation, avoiding a cramped table look. The reschedule form fields are stacked with generous vertical spacing and clear sectioning (address vs. date & time), reflecting the mockup’s roomy three-column form band. Navigation elements (breadcrumbs, buttons, CTA areas) maintain ample horizontal padding per the 16–24px pattern, and visual groupings are clear so even a long list feels browsable rather than dense.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye begins at the global header (logo and nav), then naturally transitions into the hero imagery. From the hero, attention drops to the left-aligned breadcrumbs and then to any active notification banner beneath. From there, the main Z-pattern across the content band starts at the top-left of the upcoming deliveries list (list heading and the earliest upcoming delivery), moves down through the chronological stack of deliveries, and then sweeps right toward the reschedule form column when a user focuses on changing a specific delivery. Loader states appear centered within their respective regions, briefly taking focus, while the notification banner at the top of the band provides immediate feedback after actions, pulling the eye back up momentarily before returning to the list and form.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The 'Upcoming Deliveries' page feels like a natural continuation of the corporate, service-oriented design seen in the hero + three-column order band. Below the consistent global header and full-bleed hero (with office and water imagery), a slim, full-width breadcrumbs bar grounds the user in the account area. Under that, a clean white content band stretches edge to edge, floating above a slightly tinted background and framed by generous horizontal padding matching the overall site grid. Inside this band, a broad left column lists upcoming deliveries in chronological order, each card spanning the column width with clear typography and spacious padding. To the right, a narrower but equally tall panel houses the reschedule form, styled like a focused control center with stacked fields and a bold primary action button in the brand’s orange. Notification banners briefly appear at the top of this band, using the brand’s aqua or orange to signal status without overwhelming. Loaders are minimal and centered, appearing only in-line where data is loading (either in the list area or reschedule panel). Overall, the layout guides the user smoothly from understanding their upcoming deliveries to editing a specific one, echoing the original mockup’s structured, modern, and professional feel.

layout.overall_description: The 'Upcoming Deliveries' page is organized as a vertical sequence of familiar site elements: the existing full-width navigation bar and photographic hero remain at the top, setting the brand context with the office and water imagery. Directly beneath the hero, a slim, full-width breadcrumbs bar sits on a white background, aligned with the global content width and gently separated from the hero by about 24–32px of vertical spacing. Just below this bar, a notification banner appears when needed as a colored strip spanning the same width, with comfortable 12–16px vertical padding. From there, the main white content band begins: it stretches from left to right inside the page’s max-width container and uses 24–32px internal padding on all sides. This band is divided into two columns using the implied 12-column grid: on desktop, the upcoming deliveries list occupies roughly 7–8 columns on the left, while the reschedule panel occupies about 4–5 columns on the right, separated by a generous gutter. The list column contains a heading and a vertical stack of card-like delivery items, each full-width within the column, with clear space of 16–24px between cards. The reschedule column starts at the same top baseline as the list heading; inside it, the reschedule form fields are stacked vertically with clear grouping, generous spacing, and a prominent orange action button anchored near the bottom of the panel. Loaders appear centered within their respective regions: the main loader fills the list column area when the upcoming deliveries data is loading, and a secondary loader fills the reschedule panel area when reschedule options are being fetched. Invisible state managers sit behind this layout with no visual footprint. On smaller screens, the two columns collapse into a single column flowing top-to-bottom: breadcrumbs, notification banner, list, and then the reschedule form appearing beneath or in a focused overlay triggered from a chosen list item. Throughout, the spacing adheres to the 8px base rhythm, and the palette and typography mirror the corporate, modern feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs navigation bar sits directly below the global hero area, spanning the full content width but constrained by the site’s max-width container (centered, with equal left and right margins). It forms a shallow horizontal strip approximately 48–56px tall, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 24–32px of left/right padding inside the container. The breadcrumb trail itself is left-aligned, starting near the left edge of the content grid (roughly at column 1 of the 12-column grid), and occupies only the left half of the bar; the right half remains mostly empty, maintaining the spacious, uncluttered feel. There is 16–24px of vertical space (padding or margin) separating it from the bottom of the hero, and another 8–16px of space before the notification banner or main content band below, keeping it visually distinct yet tightly associated with the page context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element, full-width within the central content container; scrolls with the page, does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification banner appears immediately beneath the breadcrumbs bar, aligned to the same max-width content container and spanning nearly the full width (approximately columns 1–12 of the grid). When visible, it forms a distinct horizontal band 48–72px tall with 12–16px of vertical padding and 24–32px horizontal padding inside, using a brand aqua or orange background for status messages. The text is left-aligned and begins at the same vertical baseline as the breadcrumbs text, reinforcing a clean, structural rhythm. There is roughly 8–12px of separation from the breadcrumbs above and 16–24px of separation from the main white content band below. When no message is present, this band collapses completely, allowing the main content band to shift upward without leaving blank gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element that appears and disappears in the normal document flow; it pushes the main content down when visible and does not overlay other components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming deliveries loader occupies the same spatial footprint as the upcoming-deliveries-list within the main content band. When active, it appears inside the left column of the band, starting at the same top baseline where the list heading would be. The loader’s container spans approximately 7–8 columns of the 12-column grid, with 24–32px padding from the left edge of the band and a similar gutter separating it from the right column. Inside this container, the loading indicator (spinner or skeleton) is centered both horizontally and vertically within a panel approximately 50–70% of the visible left-column height, with generous whitespace around it, reinforcing the airy, modern aesthetic. The right reschedule column remains visible and unchanged, but with disabled or contextual messaging as needed, allowing users to understand that the list is loading without feeling the entire page is blocked.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the upcoming-deliveries-list within the left column; it does not change the size of the column but temporarily replaces its contents while data is loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The upcoming deliveries list is the dominant content block within the main white band. On desktop, it occupies the left portion of the band, spanning roughly 7–8 of the 12 columns, starting at column 1 (or 2, depending on outer padding) and ending before a generous gutter to the reschedule form on the right. The list has 24–32px padding at the top and bottom within the band and 16–24px padding on the sides to keep items away from the edges. At the top of this column, a section title like 'Upcoming Deliveries' is aligned with the left edge of the list items. Below the title, deliveries are stacked vertically in chronological order, each list item appearing as a full-width card within the column. There is 16–24px of vertical spacing between each item, creating a comfortable rhythm. The list extends downward and becomes scrollable as the page scrolls, while the rest of the page layout remains static. On tablet, the list still appears above or alongside the form depending on breakpoints but tends to take 100% width with the form moving below. On mobile, the list becomes a single-column full-width stack beneath the breadcrumbs/notification, with the reschedule interaction likely triggered from within each item, but the visual style (card-like items, clear spacing) remains consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Block-level column within the main content band; it grows vertically with content and scrolls with the page. On wider screens it remains in a left column; on smaller screens it transitions to full-width above the reschedule form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each delivery list item is presented as a horizontal card spanning the full width of the upcoming-deliveries-list column. The card has 16–24px internal padding on all sides and a subtle border or shadow, maintaining the flat-but-elevated feel of the mockup’s form controls. The content inside the card follows a left-to-right structure: on the left, the delivery date and time window are stacked, with the date more prominent; in the center, address and order details (e.g., number of bottles) are arranged in one or two lines; on the right, a compact but visually strong 'Reschedule' button in the brand’s orange is right-aligned and vertically centered, mirroring the ORDER button emphasis in the original header. There is at least 16px of space between these internal columns, so the card never feels cramped. Vertically, cards are separated by 16–24px white space, making each delivery clearly distinct. On smaller screens, the layout reflows so that the button drops below the text and becomes full-width or near-full-width, with content stacked top-to-bottom instead of side-by-side, while preserving similar internal padding and spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeated block element within the upcoming-deliveries-list; it stretches horizontally to fill the list column, and its height adapts to content. It does not stick or float, but may visually highlight the currently selected delivery (e.g., with a subtle accent border) when linked to the reschedule form.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager has no visual representation. It operates behind the scenes, coordinating which delivery is selected, which filters or page states are active, and ensuring that the list and reschedule form reflect the state encoded in the URL. It does not occupy any space in the layout and does not alter spacing or alignment. Its presence is only evident through how the visible components (list, selected item highlight, and form contents) update as users interact with the page or share URLs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-spatial controller; it is not rendered in the DOM as a visible element and therefore has no impact on layout or flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reschedule options loader appears within the right-hand reschedule column, occupying the same space where the reschedule-form fields would normally display. When active, the loader is centered horizontally within this column and placed roughly in the upper-middle portion of the panel (about 25–40% down from the top of the band), surrounded by generous whitespace. The loader container respects the column’s padding (24–32px) and width (about 4–5 columns of the grid). The heading area of the panel (e.g., 'Reschedule Delivery') may remain visible above the loader, with the loader replacing only the input controls below, so the structure of the panel is preserved and users understand which part of the interface is waiting for data. The column height stays consistent, preventing layout jumps; only the interior contents of the form area switch to a loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the reschedule-form’s inner content; it does not change the size or position of the reschedule column, only its contents while reschedule options are being fetched.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reschedule form is housed in the right-hand column of the main white content band, visually echoing the rightmost contact column in the original mockup. On desktop, it spans approximately 4–5 of the 12 columns, starting after a generous horizontal gutter from the upcoming-deliveries-list. The panel has 24–32px internal padding, with a clear section heading at the top aligned to the left. Below the heading, form fields are stacked vertically: first address controls, then date picker, then time window, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space. Inputs are full-width within the column, using the same flat, well-padded style as the original form elements (around 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding inside each field). At the bottom of the form, a primary 'Save Changes' or 'Confirm Reschedule' button in the brand’s orange spans the full column width or at least 70–80% of it, left-aligned but visually strong, with about 16px spacing above it and 24–32px padding below before the end of the panel. The selected delivery details (e.g., date/time summary) may appear in a compact label or summary line near the top, subtly tying the form back to the currently active list item. On larger screens, this column can behave like a softly sticky panel, keeping its top aligned near the viewport top as the user scrolls through the list to the left, while still respecting the site’s padding and not overlapping the header. On smaller screens, this form collapses below the list, presenting itself as a full-width section or as a modal-style overlay triggered when the user taps 'Reschedule' on an item, but the field stacking and spacing remain consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Right-hand column within the main content band. On desktop it may use a sticky-top behavior within the viewport (sticking just below the global header when scrolled) so that the reschedule controls remain accessible while scrolling the list. On smaller breakpoints it transitions to a standard full-width block appearing beneath the list or in an overlay, scrolling with the page.

name: Order Confirmation
title: Order Confirmation
description: Displays confirmation and details for a completed order based on the orderId URL parameter, including items and scheduled delivery, and provides navigation to account and delivery management pages.

core_features: Read the orderId from the URL query string and use it to load order data via getOrderDetails(orderId).
Show a confirmation summary with order number, placement date/time, status, and total item count using the returned order object.
Display a delivery section with scheduled date, time window label, delivery method, and full delivery address using the delivery object from getOrderDetails.

List all items in the order, including quantity, item type (one-time, subscription, rental), unit price, line subtotal, and any subscription frequency or rental duration labels, using the items array from getOrderDetails.
Present an order totals block showing subtotal, discounts, total, and any applied promo code from the order data.
Indicate whether the order created subscriptions or rentals using order.contains_subscriptions and order.contains_rentals, with explanatory notes and navigation to account and upcoming deliveries.
Provide navigation actions to continue shopping (index.html), go to My Account (account.html), and view delivery schedule (upcoming_deliveries.html).
Handle loading and error states for getOrderDetails, including a retry mechanism that reuses the current orderId URL parameter.
user_workflows: Workflow 1: Arrive from checkout after placing an order: 1) User completes checkout on checkout.html, which redirects to order_confirmation.html?orderId={newOrderId}. 2) On page load, orderId is read from the URL and getOrderDetails(orderId) is called. 3) Page shows a loading state while awaiting the response. 4) On success, the confirmation summary, delivery details, item list, and totals are populated with the returned data. 5) If order.contains_subscriptions or order.contains_rentals is true, the page displays a note informing the user that these can be managed in My Account or viewed via Upcoming Deliveries. 6) User may then choose to click "Continue shopping" to go to index.html, "Go to My Account" to go to account.html, or "View delivery schedule" to go to upcoming_deliveries.html.

Workflow 2: Revisit an order confirmation directly: 1) User opens a saved link or bookmark to order_confirmation.html?orderId={existingOrderId}. 2) The page reads orderId from the URL and calls getOrderDetails(orderId). 3) Once loaded, the same confirmation, delivery, items, and totals are shown, reflecting the current data for that order. 4) User can navigate to account.html to manage related subscriptions or rentals, or to upcoming_deliveries.html to see the associated scheduled delivery.

Workflow 3: Handle missing or invalid orderId: 1) User lands on order_confirmation.html without an orderId in the query string, or with an orderId that returns an error from getOrderDetails. 2) The page detects that orderId is missing or that getOrderDetails failed. 3) An error component displays an appropriate message and shows navigation options to go to index.html or account.html. 4) If orderId is present but the call failed due to a transient error, the user can click a "Retry" action that triggers getOrderDetails again with the same orderId.
interactions: On page initialization, when order_confirmation.html is loaded, read the orderId URL parameter and trigger getOrderDetails(orderId).
If the URL query string changes to include a different orderId while the user remains on the page, trigger a fresh getOrderDetails call with the new orderId and update all bound components.
Click on a "Retry" control in the error state to call getOrderDetails again with the current orderId.
Click on "Continue shopping" to navigate the browser to index.html.
Click on "Go to My Account" to navigate the browser to account.html.
Click on "View delivery schedule" to navigate the browser to upcoming_deliveries.html.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary piece of state is the orderId URL query parameter (order_confirmation.html?orderId={orderId}). On initial load, the page reads orderId; if present, it calls getOrderDetails(orderId) and sets derived view state (loading, loaded, or error) based solely on the outcome of that call. If orderId is absent, the page immediately enters an error state and suggests navigation away from the page. The visual content of all components (order summary, delivery details, item list, totals, and subscription/rental notes) is entirely derived from the latest getOrderDetails response; no additional persistent state is stored. If the URL changes to a different orderId (for example, via manual editing or in-page navigation that updates the query string), the page re-runs getOrderDetails with the new orderId, resets to a loading state, and then updates all bound components when the new data arrives. All view variations (loading, success, error) and conditional sections (such as subscription or rental notices) are driven by fields in the getOrderDetails response (order, delivery, items) and the presence or absence of the orderId parameter, not by localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Coordinates reading the orderId from the URL and fetching data via getOrderDetails. Manages loading, success, and error states and passes the loaded order, delivery, and items data to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: On page load, read the orderId query parameter from the current URL. If present, call getOrderDetails(orderId) and set page state to loading.
onUrlParamChange: When the URL's orderId parameter changes while the user is on the page, call getOrderDetails with the new orderId and reset the loading state.
onLoadSuccess: When getOrderDetails returns successfully, update shared state with order, delivery, and items, then switch the page to the loaded state.
onLoadError: When getOrderDetails fails (missing orderId, not found, or network error), switch the page to an error state and expose error details to the error component.
onRetry: Triggered by user action from the error component; re-reads orderId from the URL and re-invokes getOrderDetails(orderId).

components.functionality: Displays the high-level confirmation information for the order, including order number, placement date/time, status, and total number of items. This component is visible only when order data is successfully loaded.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the loader component updates the bound order object after a successful getOrderDetails call, refresh the displayed order number, date/time, status, and total item count.
onUrlParamChange: If the orderId query parameter changes and a new order is loaded, clear any previous order summary and re-render with the new order data.

components.functionality: Shows delivery-specific information for the confirmed order, including scheduled date, time window label, delivery method, and full delivery address. If no delivery object is returned, this component can remain hidden or display a message based on that absence.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the loader provides a delivery object from getOrderDetails, populate the date, time window label, method, and address fields.

onMissingDelivery: If getOrderDetails returns no delivery object or null delivery fields, switch the component to a fallback state (for example, indicating that delivery details are not available) while still allowing other components to display.
