components.functionality: Renders the list of all order items using the items array returned by getOrderDetails. For each item, display product name, item type (one-time, subscription, rental), quantity, unit price, line subtotal, and any available subscription frequency or rental duration labels.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the loader component provides an updated items array, re-render the list, iterating over each item to display its details.
onItemTypeRender: For each list row, conditionally display subscription_frequency_label when item_type indicates a subscription, or rental_duration_label when item_type indicates a rental.
onUrlParamChange: When a new orderId is loaded and a new items array is returned, clear the existing list and render items from the new data set.

components.functionality: Displays monetary summary information for the order, including subtotal, discount total, final total, and any applied promo code. It reacts to updates in the order object from getOrderDetails.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the order object is updated from a successful getOrderDetails response, update the displayed subtotal, discounts, total, and promo code (if present).
onPromoPresenceChange: If promo_code is present or absent in the order data, toggle visibility of the promo summary section accordingly.

components.functionality: Informs the user when the confirmed order includes subscriptions and/or rentals and indicates that these can be managed in the account area or monitored via upcoming deliveries.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate: When the order object is refreshed, evaluate contains_subscriptions and contains_rentals to determine which informational messages to show.
onSubscriptionFlagChange: If Order.contains_subscriptions changes between false and true due to a new orderId, show or hide the subscription-specific note.
onRentalFlagChange: If Order.contains_rentals changes between false and true due to a new orderId, show or hide the rental-specific note.

components.functionality: Provides navigation controls to move away from the confirmation page after reviewing the order: continuing shopping, going to the account overview, or viewing the upcoming delivery schedule.
event_handlers: onClickContinueShopping: When the user clicks the "Continue shopping" control, navigate to index.html using a full page load.
onClickGoToAccount: When the user clicks the "Go to My Account" control, navigate to account.html using a full page load.
onClickViewDeliverySchedule: When the user clicks the "View delivery schedule" control, navigate to upcoming_deliveries.html using a full page load.

components.functionality: Handles error and edge states such as missing orderId in the URL or failures from getOrderDetails. Shows an error message, a retry option when retry is meaningful, and navigation options to move to other pages.
data_binding: getOrderDetails error state (derived from the loader component)
event_handlers: onMissingOrderId: If the URL lacks an orderId parameter on initialization, set the page to an error state and display guidance to navigate to index.html or account.html.
onGetOrderDetailsError: When the loader component reports that getOrderDetails failed for the current orderId, display an error message and show a retry control.
onClickRetry: When the user clicks the retry control, call the loader component's onRetry handler to re-invoke getOrderDetails with the current orderId from the URL.
onClickGoHome: When the user chooses to leave the error state via a home navigation control, navigate to index.html.
onClickGoToAccount: When the user chooses to go to their account from the error state, navigate to account.html.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global full-width header at the very top, then a full-bleed hero image reinforcing the professional water-delivery context, and directly beneath that a full-width white content band for the order confirmation. Inside this band, the first row uses a three-column layout (approx. 4-4-4 on a 12-column grid) for key order facts: order status, delivery details, and post-order navigation. Below that, a second row uses a two-column layout (approx. 8-4) for items and totals, followed by a full-width conditional notes strip. Error and loading states visually sit above or instead of these, but within the same general region below the hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global full-width header at the very top, then a full-bleed hero image reinforcing the professional water-delivery context, and directly beneath that a full-width white content band for the order confirmation. Inside this band, the first row uses a three-column layout (approx. 4-4-4 on a 12-column grid) for key order facts: order status, delivery details, and post-order navigation. Below that, a second row uses a two-column layout (approx. 8-4) for items and totals, followed by a full-width conditional notes strip. Error and loading states visually sit above or instead of these, but within the same general region below the hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies about 10–15% of the initial viewport height, the hero about 45–55%, and the order confirmation band beneath makes up the remaining 25–35% of the first fold. Within the confirmation band, the first three-column row takes roughly the upper half of that space, emphasizing overview and actions, while the second row for items and totals plus notes extends downward and can scroll as needed. Each column card has generous horizontal gutters (roughly 24–32px) and vertical spacing (16–24px between sections), respecting the spacious feel of the original mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and clean. High-level information (status, delivery summary, key CTAs) is surfaced in separate cards with plenty of padding (around 16–24px), mirroring the airy form band in the mockup. The items list is denser but still uses adequate row spacing and padding so it doesn't feel like a dashboard. Text is well grouped, with clear headings and short sections rather than long blocks, and there is consistent whitespace between modules to keep the layout light and scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention follows a Z-pattern: logo to navigation to the main CTA in the global header, then down into the center of the hero image. From there, the eye naturally moves down to the left-most card in the three-column confirmation band, where the order-status-summary sits as the primary confirmation cue. The gaze then tracks horizontally across to the delivery-details-section in the middle and the post-order-navigation on the right. After scanning the overview row, the user’s eye continues downward to the order-items-list on the lower left and then to the order-totals-summary on the lower right, finally landing on any subscription-rental-notes presented as a concluding strip near the bottom of the content area.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s full-width corporate header and the full-bleed hero image of the office scene with the bottled water, the page transitions into a crisp white confirmation band that fills the width of the layout. This band is aligned to an invisible 12-column grid, centered in the viewport with generous side margins, mirroring the form band from the mockup. At the top of this band, three equal-width cards sit side by side: on the left, a reassuring order status and confirmation message; in the middle, a compact block summarizing delivery address and schedule; and on the right, a stack of post-order navigation actions styled like primary and secondary CTAs. These three cards share a common top baseline and consistent vertical padding, separated by comfortable gutters, creating a balanced, professional rhythm. Beneath them, a second row spans the same width: the left two-thirds host a vertically scrollable but visually tidy list of ordered items, while the right third carries a succinct totals summary card aligned to the top of the list. Below these, when applicable, a full-width but low-height information strip communicates subscription or rental-related notes, providing closure to the content. In error or loading states, a centered overlay or prominent error panel temporarily takes over the top of this confirmation band, but the underlying grid and spacious, corporate aesthetic remain intact, preserving the look and feel of the original mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay covering the entire content area below the global header, including the hero and the main confirmation band. It stretches full-width and full-height from just under the navigation bar down to the bottom of the viewport, dimming the hero image and the confirmation content beneath. At the visual center of the viewport, a compact circular spinner or progress indicator is displayed with a short status message directly under it (e.g., 'Loading your order…'), both aligned center-center. The overlay respects the spacious design DNA by keeping the loader elements small and surrounded by ample whitespace, allowing the underlying hero image to subtly show through. On narrower viewports, the behavior is the same: the overlay pins to all four edges of the screen, with the spinner staying centered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay anchored to the viewport (position: fixed), covering the hero and confirmation band with a high z-index. It temporarily blocks interaction with underlying components until data loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-status-summary is the left-most card in the first row of the white confirmation band directly below the hero image. It occupies approximately 4 of 12 columns (about one-third of the usable content width), starting from the left margin of the grid. Its top edge aligns with the top of the delivery-details-section and post-order-navigation cards to its right, creating a clean horizontal line just under the bottom of the hero. The card has generous internal padding (around 24px) and a subtle shadow or border that distinguishes it from the background while staying flat and modern. Visually, it presents a large, bold confirmation heading ('Order Confirmed') or status badge near the top, with the order number and a short summary sentence stacked beneath, using 16–24px vertical spacing between text blocks. On smaller screens, when the layout collapses, this card stacks to the top of the content column, spanning nearly 100% width, with consistent vertical spacing above and below to maintain the open, corporate feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level card participating in the three-column grid row; it reflows responsively to a full-width card when the viewport is too narrow for three columns.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-details-section sits immediately to the right of the order-status-summary within the same horizontal row. It occupies the center 4 columns of the 12-column grid, with equal horizontal gutters on both sides (roughly 24–32px between adjacent cards). Its top aligns with the top of the status summary and post-order navigation, and its height is dictated by the amount of delivery info but generally similar to or slightly shorter than the status card. Inside, content is stacked vertically: a concise 'Delivery Details' heading at the top, followed by grouped information blocks (address, date, time window, delivery instructions), each separated by 8–16px of vertical spacing. The overall card width and typography mirror the order-status-summary for visual cohesion. On mobile, it drops below the order-status-summary card, spanning the full content width, maintaining at least 24px of vertical spacing from the card above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card participating in the first three-column row; transitions to a full-width stacked card under the order-status-summary on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-order-navigation component occupies the right-most 4 columns of the first row in the confirmation band, mirroring the width and vertical alignment of the two cards to its left. It sits flush with the right grid margin, with a gutter separating it from the delivery-details-section. The card interior is arranged as a vertical stack of navigation options: at the top, a prominent orange primary button (e.g., 'Manage Deliveries') that stretches most of the card width with approximately 10–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding; beneath it, one or two secondary text links or ghost buttons ('View Account', 'Back to Shop') aligned left, each separated by around 12–16px. The surrounding padding (about 24px on all sides) and clear vertical spacing ensure the nav area feels focused but not crowded. On small screens, this navigation card stacks beneath the delivery-details-section as a full-width module, with its main button remaining visually strong and centered horizontally within the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation card. It reflows from the right column of the three-column row into a full-width block at the bottom of that stack on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-details-section sits immediately to the right of the order-status-summary within the same horizontal row. It occupies the center 4 columns of the 12-column grid, with equal horizontal gutters on both sides (roughly 24–32px between adjacent cards). Its top aligns with the top of the status summary and post-order navigation, and its height is dictated by the amount of delivery info but generally similar to or slightly shorter than the status card. Inside, content is stacked vertically: a concise 'Delivery Details' heading at the top, followed by grouped information blocks (address, date, time window, delivery instructions), each separated by 8–16px of vertical spacing. The overall card width and typography mirror the order-status-summary for visual cohesion. On mobile, it drops below the order-status-summary card, spanning the full content width, maintaining at least 24px of vertical spacing from the card above and below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card participating in the first three-column row; transitions to a full-width stacked card under the order-status-summary on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-items-list begins a second content band immediately below the three-column row of summary, delivery, and navigation cards. It starts with a top margin of about 32px from the bottom of the row above to preserve the spacious hierarchy. This list occupies roughly 8 of 12 columns (two-thirds width) on the left side of the grid. Its top edge aligns with the top of the order-totals-summary card to its right. Within the items list card, a section title (e.g., 'Items in Your Order') sits at the top left, followed by a vertically stacked list of item rows. Each row is horizontally laid out (thumbnail or icon, product name, quantity, and line price) with comfortable vertical spacing (12–16px) between rows and ample left/right padding (16–24px). The list area may scroll within the page if very long, but there is no internal scroll container; instead, the page itself extends downward. On smaller screens, this component expands to full width and moves above the order-totals-summary card, maintaining a 24–32px gap from the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block: left column of the second row. It reflows to a full-width block preceding the totals card in the vertical stack on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-totals-summary is positioned to the right of the order-items-list within the same horizontal band. It occupies the remaining 4 columns of the grid on the right side, aligning its top edge with the 'Items in Your Order' heading to create a cohesive row. The card is narrower but visually dense, with a clear 'Order Total' heading followed by a vertical stack of subtotal, fees, discounts, and final total. Each line is separated by 8–12px, with the final total given extra emphasis and slightly more spacing above. Internal padding mirrors the other cards (around 24px), and the card stands out subtly via a border or light shadow. On small screens, this card drops below the order-items-list and spans nearly the full width, keeping at least 24px of vertical separation from the list for clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static card in the right column of the second row; reflows under the order-items-list as a full-width block in single-column mobile layouts.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The subscription-rental-notes component appears only when relevant and is placed below the band that contains the order-items-list and order-totals-summary. It is presented as a low-height, full-width strip that spans the main content width but is visually centered within the grid, with side margins consistent with the cards above. There is a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px between the bottom of the totals/items row and the top of this strip, allowing it to read as a separate but related section. The strip uses a subtle tinted background (e.g., pale aqua) and possibly a small icon on the left, with one or two short lines of text explaining subscription or rental terms aligned to the left and vertically centered within the bar. Padding inside the strip is moderate (16–20px vertically, 24px horizontally), maintaining the airy feel. On mobile, it remains a full-width bar beneath the stacked items and totals, preserving the same spacing relationships.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline informational strip at the bottom of the confirmation content. It occupies a full-width row after the second content band and moves naturally with the page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-order-navigation component occupies the right-most 4 columns of the first row in the confirmation band, mirroring the width and vertical alignment of the two cards to its left. It sits flush with the right grid margin, with a gutter separating it from the delivery-details-section. The card interior is arranged as a vertical stack of navigation options: at the top, a prominent orange primary button (e.g., 'Manage Deliveries') that stretches most of the card width with approximately 10–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding; beneath it, one or two secondary text links or ghost buttons ('View Account', 'Back to Shop') aligned left, each separated by around 12–16px. The surrounding padding (about 24px on all sides) and clear vertical spacing ensure the nav area feels focused but not crowded. On small screens, this navigation card stacks beneath the delivery-details-section as a full-width module, with its main button remaining visually strong and centered horizontally within the content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation card. It reflows from the right column of the three-column row into a full-width block at the bottom of that stack on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-confirmation-error-panel is positioned at the very top of the white confirmation band, directly below the hero image and above the three-column row of status, delivery, and navigation. When present, it spans the central portion of the grid, typically 6–8 columns wide, and is horizontally centered so that there are generous left and right margins, making it feel like a focused alert. The top of the panel sits about 24–32px below the bottom edge of the hero, maintaining consistent section spacing. Inside the panel, a bold error title appears on the top line, with a concise explanation and possible retry or support link beneath, each separated by 8–12px. The panel’s background uses a light warning or error tint, with perhaps a thin colored border, but keeps typography and spacing consistent with the brand’s corporate style. When the error panel is visible, the rest of the confirmation content either appears dimmed beneath or is pushed down, but the user’s eye is clearly drawn first to this panel before scanning anything else. On small screens, the panel remains full-width within the content area, with the same top spacing below the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static alert block inserted at the top of the confirmation content stack. It does not float; it pushes the other components downward when displayed. In more critical flows, it could optionally be combined with a subtle scroll-into-view behavior when mounted.

name: About
title: About
description: Displays dynamically loaded company and service information for the bottled water delivery service, including mission, service area, sustainability efforts, and quality standards.

core_features: Load and display About page content (title, body, last updated) via getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug derived for this page.
Render the informational HTML body returned by the interface, including headings, paragraphs, links, and any embedded calls to action.
Show the last updated timestamp of the About content for transparency and trust.
Support deep-linking to specific sections of the About content via URL parameters (e.g., about.html?section=mission) and scroll to the corresponding anchor in the rendered HTML if present.
Provide navigation from the About content to the Contact page when the user clicks a designated “Contact us” call-to-action link.
Provide navigation back to the homepage when the user clicks the site logo in the header.
user_workflows:
View general About information:
1. User navigates to about.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2. On page load, the page determines the pageSlug for this page (e.g., "about").
3. The page calls getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug).
4. The interface returns an object containing title, body_html, and last_updated.
5. The page sets the document title to the returned title and renders body_html into the main content area.
6. The page displays last_updated in a small metadata area (e.g., “Last updated” label) without modifying its value.
7. User reads the content and can scroll normally.

Open About page to a specific section:
1. User opens about.html with a query parameter specifying section (e.g., about.html?section=mission), either by clicking an internal link or entering the URL directly.
2. On page load, the page parses the URL query parameters and reads the section parameter value.
3. The page calls getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug) as usual.
4. After body_html is rendered, the page looks for a matching anchor or element whose id corresponds to the section parameter value.
5. If a matching element is found, the page programmatically scrolls to that element and sets accessible focus (if appropriate).
6. If no matching element is found, the page leaves the scroll position at the top without raising an error to the user.

Navigate from About to Contact page via CTA:
1. User is viewing the About content that has been rendered from body_html.
2. User clicks a "Contact us" CTA link embedded in the body_html or provided as a separate functional component.
3. The click handler prevents any default behavior that conflicts with intended navigation logic (if necessary) and navigates the browser to contact.html, optionally including a URL parameter such as contact.html?from=about to preserve the source context.
4. The Contact page loads separately and can use the from parameter if needed (outside the scope of this page).

Navigate from About to homepage via logo:
1. User is on about.html.
2. User clicks the site logo in the header component.
3. The click event triggers navigation to index.html without modifying other URL parameters for this page.
4. The homepage loads separately (outside the scope of this page).

Change section via in-page navigation links:
1. While on about.html, user clicks an in-page navigation link that points to another section, implemented as a link updating the section parameter (e.g., about.html?section=sustainability).
2. The browser navigates to the new URL with the updated section parameter.
3. The page re-runs the load sequence: parse URL, call getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug), re-render title and body_html.
4. After rendering, the page scrolls to the element corresponding to the new section parameter if it exists.
interactions: On initial page load of about.html, the page automatically calls getInformationalPageContent with the configured pageSlug associated to this page and renders the returned content.
User scrolls through the About content; scrolling does not trigger additional data calls and only affects viewport position.

User clicks a "Contact us" CTA link inside the rendered body_html or in a dedicated component; the page navigates to contact.html, optionally appending a query parameter indicating the origin (e.g., from=about).
User clicks the site logo in the header; the page navigates to index.html.

User clicks an in-page navigation link that updates the URL (e.g., about.html?section=quality); the browser reloads or updates the page, which then scrolls to the corresponding section after re-rendering.

If the About content is reloaded due to a navigation that stays on about.html but changes the query string, the page re-applies the section scrolling logic without making extra interface calls beyond what is needed for the pageSlug.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver for this page is the URL. The path about.html indicates this is the About page. Internally, the page uses a fixed logical slug (e.g., "about") as pageSlug when calling getInformationalPageContent; the slug value is a configuration for this page, not user-editable state. Query parameters are used to represent sub-state:

- section: Optional. Example: about.html?section=mission. When present, after the content from getInformationalPageContent is rendered, the page attempts to find an element within the rendered body_html whose id matches the section value. If found, the page scrolls to that element and may move keyboard focus there. If not found, no error is shown and the page remains at the default scroll position.
- from: Optional. Not used in data loading on this page, but may be appended when navigating away, for example when constructing contact.html?from=about from the Contact CTA. This parameter is only written when the page navigates away; the About page itself does not react to from.

The page does not maintain application state in localStorage or other client-side storage. All content is derived from the current URL and the response of getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug). Whenever the path remains about.html but query parameters change (for example, a different section), the page reads the updated URL parameters, calls getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug) as needed for this page, re-renders the content, and re-applies the scrolling and focus behavior. No other data models are read or mutated by this page.

components.functionality: On page initialization and whenever about.html is loaded, this component determines the appropriate pageSlug for the About page and calls getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug). It passes the returned title, body_html, and last_updated to downstream components for rendering.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse the current URL to confirm this is about.html, set pageSlug for this page, call getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug), then dispatch the response data to listening components (e.g., content renderer and metadata renderer).

onUrlChangeWithinPage: If the application framework allows handling query parameter changes without full reload, re-parse the URL, ensure pageSlug remains the same, and if necessary re-call getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug) before notifying components to apply section-specific behavior.

components.functionality: Render the informational content of the About page using the structured data provided by about-content-loader. It sets the document title, injects the HTML body into the main content region, and shows the last updated timestamp.
event_handlers: onContentDataReceived: When title, body_html, and last_updated are received from about-content-loader, set the browser document.title to the returned title; render body_html into the main content container using safe HTML injection; render last_updated in a metadata area.

onReRenderRequested: When notified that the URL changed but the page is still about.html, update the rendered content based on the latest getInformationalPageContent response and trigger the section-scrolling component.

components.functionality: Implements deep-linking behavior by reading the section query parameter from the URL and scrolling/focusing the corresponding element in the rendered About content if it exists.
event_handlers: onContentRendered: After about-content-renderer finishes injecting body_html, read the section parameter from the URL. If a section value exists, attempt to find an element with id equal to that value inside the rendered content. If found, scroll that element into view and optionally set keyboard focus for accessibility.

onUrlChangeWithinPage: When the query string changes (e.g., user navigates to about.html?section=quality), re-run the section lookup and scrolling logic after the content is confirmed rendered. If no matching element is found, do nothing further.

components.functionality: Handles navigation from the About page to the Contact page when the user activates a designated Contact CTA link. This CTA can originate in body_html or be rendered as a separate link component.
event_handlers: onClick: For the specific "Contact us" CTA link or links designated for contacting support, intercept the click if necessary and navigate to contact.html, optionally appending a query parameter such as ?from=about to reflect the source page. If the link is already defined in body_html with an href pointing to contact.html or contact.html?from=about, the handler can allow the default navigation while still ensuring consistent behavior across implementations.
onKeyActivate: Support keyboard activation (e.g., Enter or Space depending on element type) on the CTA element and perform the same navigation to contact.html.

components.functionality: Provides navigation back to the homepage when the user clicks the site logo from the About page header.
event_handlers: onClick: When the logo element is clicked, navigate the browser to index.html. This does not depend on or modify any interface data.
onKeyActivate: When the logo element is focused and activated via keyboard (e.g., Enter), navigate to index.html.

components.functionality: Ensures the footer "About" link correctly points to this page and can also incorporate a section parameter when needed.
event_handlers: onClick: When the footer About link is clicked from any other page, navigate to about.html, optionally without additional parameters unless specifically provided (e.g., about.html?section=value when linking to a sub-section). The About page then handles loading content through getInformationalPageContent(pageSlug).

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: a full‑width header bar at the very top; a full‑bleed hero image directly beneath, visually dominating the first screen; then a full‑width white content band for the About information, structured into three vertical columns on a 12‑column grid. In this band, the left column hosts in‑page navigation (section scroller), the middle column displays the dynamically loaded About content, and the right column provides a condensed contact/CTA block. Below this, the page continues with vertically stacked About subsections in the center column, ending with a full‑width footer that includes the About footer navigation link.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top‑to‑bottom structure: a full‑width header bar at the very top; a full‑bleed hero image directly beneath, visually dominating the first screen; then a full‑width white content band for the About information, structured into three vertical columns on a 12‑column grid. In this band, the left column hosts in‑page navigation (section scroller), the middle column displays the dynamically loaded About content, and the right column provides a condensed contact/CTA block. Below this, the page continues with vertically stacked About subsections in the center column, ending with a full‑width footer that includes the About footer navigation link.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a shallow strip of roughly 10–12% of the initial viewport height. The hero image consumes around 45–55% of the first viewport, extending edge‑to‑edge. The initial visible portion of the About content band fills the remaining ~25–35% of the viewport, with enough vertical depth that it continues below the fold. Within that band, the three columns each use approximately one third of the content width (4 of 12 columns), separated by generous horizontal gutters (roughly 24–32px). Vertical spacing between form‑like elements and text blocks follows the 16–24px rhythm, with section‑level padding of 32–48px above and below.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and airy, mirroring the mockup: no element feels cramped, and each block of content has enough whitespace to breathe. The hero section is visually spacious with a focus on photography rather than text. The About content in the center column is broken into clearly separated sections (mission, service area, sustainability, quality), each with comfortable padding and 16–24px spacing between headings and paragraphs. The side columns (section scroller and contact CTA) are succinct and vertically separated so they feel like concise utilities rather than dense sidebars.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the mockup’s Z‑pattern and top‑down guidance. The eye starts at the logo on the top‑left of the header, moves across the navigation to the right‑aligned primary CTA. From there, the user’s gaze naturally drops into the middle of the hero image where the branded water bottle is central. The line of sight then shifts rightward along the office imagery, reinforcing professionalism. As the user scrolls down, attention lands on the three‑column About band, starting at the left with the in‑page section navigation, then moving to the central About content, and finally to the right‑aligned contact CTA. As the user scrolls further, the central column’s stacked About sections lead vertically down to the footer, where the About footer link sits with other navigation items, closing the flow.

layout.overall_description: The About page opens with a full‑width, white header bar pinned to the very top of the viewport, containing the company logo on the left, a row of navigation links across the center, and a brightly colored aqua or orange CTA button aligned to the right. Immediately beneath, a full‑bleed hero image fills nearly half the screen height: a clean office scene with a prominent water bottle and glass in the foreground, subtly overlaid in the center‑left with a concise 'About Our Service' title and short summary line in modern sans‑serif type. As the user scrolls past the hero, a crisp white content band stretches from edge to edge. This band is organized into three equal‑width columns within a 12‑column grid, leaving comfortable gutters between each. The left column presents a vertical list of in‑page navigation links for About sections; the middle column shows the dynamically loaded About content—mission, service area, sustainability, and quality—stacked vertically, each block generously spaced; the right column presents customer service hours and a prominent phone number plus a contact CTA styled similarly to the header button. Below this band, the central column continues the About narrative in vertically layered sections, while the left and right columns either taper off or become less prominent depending on screen height. The page concludes with a balanced, full‑width footer that echoes the header’s visual simplicity, within which the About footer navigation link sits alongside other site links, aligned in a horizontal row with comfortable padding. Throughout, the layout is flat and modern, with clear hierarchy, consistent 8px‑based spacing, and a steady left‑to‑right, then top‑to‑bottom reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader component is conceptually anchored to the central column of the white About content band, occupying the same footprint as the About content renderer. When the About page first loads, a subtle loading treatment (such as skeleton text blocks or a simple spinner) appears centered within the middle column, starting roughly 32–48px below the bottom edge of the hero section. It spans the width of that central column (approximately 1/3 of the total content width, or 4 of 12 grid columns), with 24–32px of padding on the left and right to preserve the airy feel. Vertically, it occupies just enough height to signal loading without overpowering the layout, leaving at least 32px of whitespace above the loader and similar spacing below before any additional content or columns begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is positioned in normal document flow within the central column of the three‑column About band, appearing only while data is being fetched and disappearing in place once content is loaded; it does not fix or float relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The About content renderer dominates the central column of the white About band that appears directly beneath the hero image. This central column begins roughly 32–48px below the hero’s bottom edge and spans about one third of the page’s content width, aligned centrally on the grid (4 of 12 columns), with 24–32px horizontal gutters separating it from the left and right columns. Within this column, the renderer outputs distinct sections—such as Mission, Service Area, Sustainability Efforts, and Quality Standards—stacked vertically. Each section starts with a bold, left‑aligned heading, followed by body copy that spans the full width of the column, with 16–24px vertical spacing between headings and paragraphs and 32–40px between major sections. The text locks to the left edge of the column to align visually with the left in‑page navigation links. As the user scrolls, this central content continues down the page, extending beyond the initial three‑column band while maintaining the same column width and generous vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It flows naturally in the main document stream, scrolling with the page; its width remains tied to the central grid column, adapting responsively but always appearing as the main content spine below the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The in‑page navigation (section scroller) sits in the left column of the three‑column About band, aligned vertically with the top of the About content in the middle column. This left column occupies roughly one third of the content area (4 of 12 grid columns), beginning around 32–48px below the hero image. Inside this column, a compact, vertically stacked list of section links—e.g., 'Our Mission', 'Service Area', 'Sustainability', 'Quality & Safety'—is left‑aligned, with each item separated by 12–16px of vertical space. The column has 24–32px of padding from the left page edge, mirroring the spacious navigation style from the header. The overall height of the scroller is modest so it feels concise and utility‑like, and it sits with 24–32px of whitespace below it before any other potential content in that column. On larger screens, as the user scrolls, the scroller remains visibly near the top portion of the viewport so the user can easily jump between sections without conflicting with the hero or footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is positioned within the left column in normal flow, but may use a light sticky behavior on larger viewports—sticking near the top of the viewport once the user scrolls past its initial position, while respecting the header at the very top—to keep in‑page navigation persistently accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact CTA handler occupies the rightmost column of the three‑column About band, horizontally aligned with the top edges of the left section scroller and central content. This right column takes up roughly one third of the page’s content width (4 of 12 grid columns) with a 24–32px gutter separating it from the central column. Within this column, a small text block at the top presents customer service context—such as 'Customer Service' and 'Mon–Fri, 8am–6pm'—stacked as one or two short lines with 8–12px vertical spacing. Directly beneath, a larger, high‑contrast phone number is rendered in a bolder weight and slightly larger size, drawing the eye, with 16–24px of space separating it from the preceding text. Below the number, a button‑style CTA (e.g., 'Contact Us' or 'Schedule a Delivery') spans about 70–80% of the column width, left‑aligned or centered within the column, with internal padding around 8–10px vertically and 16–20px horizontally, following the mockup’s button sizing. There is at least 24–32px of padding from the top of this column to the first line of text and similar padding below the last element to keep this block feeling light and approachable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It remains within the normal document flow of the right column, scrolling off screen as the user moves down the page; it does not fix to the viewport, but its vertically compact design ensures it is fully visible within the initial view of the About band on most screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header logo and navigation component forms a full‑width horizontal bar pinned to the very top of the page, visually separating the browser chrome from the content. Its height is a shallow strip—approximately 72–88px on desktop (about 10–12% of viewport height)—with a clean white or very light background to contrast with the colorful hero image beneath. On the far left, the company logo is placed with 24–32px of horizontal padding from the edge. To the right of the logo, centered vertically in the bar, a row of navigation links stretches toward the middle of the header, each link spaced with 16–24px horizontal padding to maintain the airy feel. On the far right, aligned flush with 24–32px padding from the edge, a brightly colored aqua/orange CTA button (e.g., 'Order Water') stands out, consistent with the brand palette. The 'About' navigation link appears among these items and is styled to indicate the current page (e.g., bolder text or a subtle underline), but does not overshadow the main CTA. The entire header aligns with the same underlying grid and sets the initial visual hierarchy, leading directly into the hero image below with minimal vertical gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is fixed to the top of the viewport on scroll to maintain constant access to primary navigation and CTA, with the rest of the page content scrolling beneath it; the header height remains constant, preserving a stable frame for the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The About footer navigation link resides within the site’s full‑width footer at the very bottom of the page, visually mirroring the simplicity and alignment of the header but with a more subdued presence. The footer is a horizontal band with a height of roughly 80–120px, padded vertically by 24–32px, and spans edge‑to‑edge with a light gray or muted background that subtly contrasts with the white content areas above. Inside this band, a row of footer navigation links—including the About link—is aligned either to the left or center, following the site’s overall footer pattern. Each link is given 16–24px of horizontal spacing to stay legible and uncluttered. The About link is styled consistently with other footer links (a simple text link), possibly indicating the current page with a subtle color variation but remaining visually understated. This row is placed above any legal text or small print, with 12–16px of vertical spacing separating them, and maintains alignment with the same 12‑column content width used above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: It is part of the footer’s normal flow, appearing only when the user scrolls to the bottom of the page; it is not sticky or fixed and simply anchors the end of the vertical layout.

name: Contact
title: Contact
description: Page where users can submit contact or support requests and view current support contact details and service hours.

core_features: Load and display current support contact details and service hours using getContactPageInfo.
Provide a contact form that allows users to submit a request using submitContactRequest.
Populate a selectable list of contact reasons from the contact_reasons array returned by getContactPageInfo and allow preselection via URL parameters.
Allow users to navigate to the Help Center (help_center.html) with context derived from the current contact reason and form state using URL parameters.
Show inline confirmation or error feedback after a contact request is submitted, using the response from submitContactRequest.
user_workflows: Workflow: Submit a new contact request from scratch; user opens contact.html (without query parameters) -> page calls getContactPageInfo -> page initializes form state (including default contact reason based on contact_reasons from API) -> user fills in name, email, subject, message, preferred contact method, and optionally phone -> user submits form -> page sends submitContactRequest with form field values -> on success, page shows confirmation and ticket_id from the API response; on failure, page shows the error message from the API response.

Workflow: Submit a context-specific request via URL parameters; user is directed to contact.html with query parameters like reason, subject, email, and message -> page calls getContactPageInfo -> when data is loaded, page matches the reason parameter to one of the values from contact_reasons (if found) and sets it as the selected reason; subject/email/message fields are initialized from their respective URL parameters if present -> user reviews/edits the prefilled fields -> user submits the form -> submitContactRequest is called with the edited values -> page shows confirmation or error based on the response.

Workflow: Move from the Contact page to the Help Center; user opens contact.html -> page calls getContactPageInfo and displays contact details and the form -> user optionally selects a contact reason and possibly edits subject/message -> user clicks the "Visit Help Center" action -> page constructs a URL to help_center.html with query parameters (for example, from=contact and reason matching the currently selected contact reason, and optionally subject) -> browser navigates to that URL, allowing the Help Center page to use those parameters for contextual help.

Workflow: Navigate back to the home page from Contact; user is on contact.html -> user clicks the site logo in the header -> browser navigates to index.html (no API call from this page is required for this navigation).
interactions: Interaction: Page load; on initial render of contact.html, a controller component triggers getContactPageInfo with no parameters; when the promise resolves, support_email, support_phone, service_hours, and contact_reasons are stored in page state and passed to child components to render contact details and initialize form options.

Interaction: Reason preselection via URL; after getContactPageInfo resolves, the page reads the reason query parameter from the URL; if its value matches one of the entries in contact_reasons, that entry is set as the selected reason in the form; if not, the form uses a default selection based on the loaded contact_reasons (e.g., the first element) without hardcoding a specific value.

Interaction: Form field prefill via URL; on load, the page reads query parameters such as name, email, subject, message, and preferredContactMethod (if present) and initializes the corresponding form fields with those values; users can edit these values before submitting.

Interaction: Form input changes; when the user types or changes any form field (name, email, subject, message, preferredContactMethod, phone, or selected reason), the form component updates its internal state; this state is not written to localStorage but may optionally be reflected in the URL via history updates (e.g., updating the reason parameter when the selected reason changes).

Interaction: Form submission; when the user submits the contact form, the form validates that required fields contain non-empty values and that the email has a syntactically valid format; if validation passes, the component calls submitContactRequest with name, email, subject (possibly augmented with the selected reason), message, preferredContactMethod, and phone; the submit button enters a loading state until the request resolves.

Interaction: Submission success; if submitContactRequest returns success=true, the page displays a confirmation state using the returned message and ticket_id; the confirmation is shown inline on the same contact.html page and can disable or reset the form fields depending on configuration; the page does not navigate away automatically.

Interaction: Submission failure; if submitContactRequest returns success=false, the page displays the returned message as an error state and keeps the form values intact for user correction; the user can edit fields and resubmit.

Interaction: Visit Help Center link; when the user clicks the "Visit Help Center" control from the Contact page, the click handler reads the current selected reason and possibly subject from form state and constructs a URL to help_center.html with query parameters (for example, reason and subject); the browser then navigates to that URL.
Interaction: Click site logo; when the user clicks the logo in the shared header, the browser navigates to index.html; form state is not preserved and no additional interface calls are made.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Contact page uses URL query parameters on contact.html to manage and restore state without relying on localStorage. On initial load, the page parses parameters such as reason, name, email, subject, message, and preferredContactMethod. After getContactPageInfo resolves, the reason parameter is matched against values in contact_reasons; if a match is found, that reason is selected in the form; otherwise, the selected reason falls back to a non-hardcoded default from the returned contact_reasons. The name, email, subject, message, and preferredContactMethod parameters, if present, are used as the initial values of the corresponding form fields. The page may update the URL using the browser history API when the selected contact reason changes, so that the reason parameter reflects the current selection; similarly, subject or other fields can be synchronized to the URL as needed for deep linking. After a successful form submission, the success state (including ticket_id and confirmation message) is stored in in-memory page state; the confirmation is shown immediately without navigating or modifying data models. Navigation from this page to help_center.html includes query parameters such as from=contact and the current reason and subject values, allowing the Help Center page to tailor its content; navigation to index.html via the logo uses a simple index.html URL with no additional parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading for the Contact page, manages URL-based state (reason and field prefills), and supplies props to child components such as the contact details panel and contact form.

Displays current support contact details (such as email, phone) and service hours, using the values returned by getContactPageInfo; may expose click actions such as initiating a call or creating an email via the user’s client.

Collects user inputs required to create a contact request, including name, email, subject, message, preferredContactMethod, phone, and a selected reason derived from contact_reasons; validates the inputs and calls submitContactRequest to submit the request; handles success and error responses and shows appropriate inline feedback.

Shows the current status of the contact request submission, including success messages and ticket_id or error messages, based solely on the response from submitContactRequest; this component appears within the Contact page without causing navigation.

Provides a "Visit Help Center" action from the Contact page that builds a contextual URL to help_center.html using URL parameters derived from the current selected contact reason and, optionally, the current subject; it then navigates the browser to that URL.
Located in the shared header; when activated on the Contact page, it navigates directly to index.html without modifying or depending on Contact page data.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom page flow that mirrors the mockup: a full-width header bar at the very top containing the logo on the left and navigation (including the Help Center link) on the right; immediately below, a full-bleed hero image that sets the professional, service-oriented context; directly beneath the hero, an optional full-width submission feedback banner; and finally a white contact band that spans the full page width and houses the main contact form on the left and contact details on the right, organized on a 12-column responsive grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom page flow that mirrors the mockup: a full-width header bar at the very top containing the logo on the left and navigation (including the Help Center link) on the right; immediately below, a full-bleed hero image that sets the professional, service-oriented context; directly beneath the hero, an optional full-width submission feedback banner; and finally a white contact band that spans the full page width and houses the main contact form on the left and contact details on the right, organized on a 12-column responsive grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a shallow strip of around 10–15% of the initial viewport height. The hero image takes up roughly 45–55% of the first viewport to dominate the initial impression. The contact area (form + details) fills the remaining 25–35% of the first fold, but can vertically expand with form content. Within this contact band, the form occupies about two-thirds of the content width (≈8 of 12 columns), and the contact-details panel occupies the remaining one-third (≈4 of 12 columns), aligning to the mockup’s three-column rhythm. Section padding and vertical spacing follow the 8px scale, with 32–48px vertical padding per major section.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain moderate, breathable density. Navigation items in the header are spaced with generous horizontal padding so the bar feels clean. The hero is largely imagery with minimal overlaid content, giving a spacious, corporate feel. In the contact band, inputs are comfortably spaced with about 16–24px vertical gaps, and generous side gutters between the form and the contact details. The contact-details panel uses short text blocks, clear line spacing, and plenty of white space to keep information scannable rather than dense.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow begins at the top-left logo, moves horizontally across to the right-side navigation where the Help Center link sits among other items, echoing the mockup’s Z-pattern. The eye then drops to the center of the hero image (bottle/glass focal point), travels slightly right following the implied professional context, and then moves down into the contact band. Within the band, the eye starts on the left with the contact form fields, proceeds through the vertically stacked inputs, and then naturally shifts to the right to read the contact details and service hours. If visible, the submission feedback banner acts as a temporary, high-contrast strip that the eye catches first when revisiting the page after a submission, before naturally returning to the form and details below.

layout.overall_description: The Contact page is structured as a clean, corporate, top-to-bottom layout that strongly echoes the original bottled water service mockup. A full-width header bar anchors the top of the page, with the brand logo linked on the left and a row of navigation links on the right, including a clearly legible Help Center link. Below, a full-bleed hero image fills roughly half the screen, showing the water bottle and glass in the foreground against a professional office backdrop, reinforcing the service’s reliability. Directly underneath the hero, an optional, slim submission feedback banner spans edge to edge, using brand color to stand out while still feeling integrated. Beneath that, a white contact band occupies the remaining initial viewport: the left two-thirds host the main contact form, laid out in a balanced, multi-field arrangement, while the right one-third contains a neatly stacked contact-details panel with service hours and support contact information. Generous horizontal gutters separate these left and right zones, and all fields and lines of text are spaced using the 8px scale so nothing feels cramped. The overall effect is of a modern, professional service page where the hero establishes brand and context, and the lower band clearly invites the user to get in touch or seek help.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-page-controller acts as the structural container for the entire Contact page, defining a full-width, vertically stacked layout that adheres to a 12-column responsive grid. At the very top of the viewport, it positions the header/navigation bar as a horizontal strip spanning 100% of the window width. Immediately below that, it allocates a full-bleed region for the hero image, ensuring the image stretches from the left edge to the right edge and occupies roughly the upper half of the screen on desktop. Directly under the hero region, it reserves a narrow, full-width slot for the submission-feedback-banner; when not in use, this space collapses so that the contact band sits directly under the hero. The lower part of the controller is taken by the contact band, which is a white, full-width section with generous top and bottom padding (around 32–48px) and centered content constrained to a maximum width (e.g., 1080–1200px). Within this content width, the controller establishes a three-part grid rhythm: the left two-thirds (≈8 columns) house the contact-form component, subdividable into internal columns; the right one-third (≈4 columns) houses the contact-details-panel. On smaller screens, the controller reflows these components into a single column, stacking the contact-form above the contact-details-panel, while preserving vertical spacing consistent with the 8px-based scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the standard document flow; all contained sections scroll normally with the page, respecting the top-to-bottom stacking order without fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-details-panel is positioned within the main contact band at the lower portion of the page, occupying the rightmost third of the central content area. It aligns to the top of the contact-form on its left so that both the form and the details panel start on the same horizontal baseline. On a 12-column grid, this panel spans approximately 4 columns, with a generous gutter (24–32px) separating it from the form area. The panel itself is set on the same white background as the contact band for a unified look, but can be visually framed by slightly increased internal padding (around 24–32px) and subtle typographic hierarchy: a bold or slightly larger heading at the top (e.g., “Customer Support”), followed by one or two short paragraphs or bullet lines for service hours, and then a prominent support phone number or email. Each line of text is vertically spaced by about 12–16px to maintain legibility and a light feel. At the bottom of this panel, there is room for a short, secondary line of supporting text (e.g., “Response time within 1 business day”), spaced 16–24px below the main contact details. On mobile or narrow viewports, this panel drops below the contact-form, spanning nearly 100% of the width, with the same internal spacing but increased top margin to clearly separate it from the form above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block within the contact band; it maintains its right-aligned position on wider screens and gracefully stacks under the contact-form on smaller screens without fixed or sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form is the main interactive element within the contact band and sits on the left side, occupying about two-thirds of the central content width. Using the 12-column grid, it spans approximately 8 columns, starting from the left margin of the content area. In keeping with the mockup’s three-column order/contact pattern, the form is visually structured into two vertical sub-columns within its area: for example, the left sub-column might contain fields like “Name” and “Customer Type,” while the right sub-column contains “Email” and “Phone,” with a wider, full-row textarea (e.g., “How can we help?”) spanning both sub-columns below them. Each input has generous internal padding (around 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal) and is separated from the next by about 16–24px of vertical spacing. The two sub-columns are divided by a consistent gutter (24–32px) to avoid crowding. A primary submit button (e.g., “Send Request”) is aligned along the bottom-left of the form area, sitting 24–32px below the last field and visually associated with the form through alignment and proximity. The entire form block has comfortable top padding relative to the contact band’s top edge, aligning horizontally with the contact-details-panel title on the right. On smaller screens, the sub-columns collapse into a single vertical stack, with all fields spanning nearly the full width of the content area; spacing between fields remains consistent with the 8px-based scale so the form still feels breathable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow within the contact band; its internal layout adapts responsively from a two-column to a single-column arrangement as the viewport narrows, without fixed or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-feedback-banner appears as a slim, full-width horizontal strip directly beneath the hero image and immediately above the contact band. When active (e.g., after a successful or failed form submission), it spans the entire browser width edge to edge, visually separating the hero and the contact section. Its height is modest—roughly equivalent to 48–64px—yet it stands out using one of the brand’s high-contrast colors (for instance, aqua for success, soft orange for warnings or errors) with white or dark text for legibility. The content inside the banner is horizontally centered within the same max-width container as the contact band, aligning left with the contact-form’s left edge and right with the contact-details-panel’s right edge. The banner contains a brief message line and optionally an icon to the left, with about 16px padding above and below the message. There is at least 16–24px of vertical separation between the bottom of this banner and the top of the contact band, achieved via padding or a subtle shadow, so it reads as its own horizontal slice. When inactive, the banner collapses entirely, allowing the contact band to sit directly beneath the hero with no leftover gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered in the standard document flow; when shown, it pushes the contact band downward, rather than overlaying, and scrolls away with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-center-link is placed within the top navigation bar, aligned to the right side to mirror the mockup’s right-weighted menu and CTA pattern. Within the header, it appears as one of the navigation items in a horizontal row, positioned toward the far right but slightly left of any primary CTA button (such as an implied “Order” button), so it is clearly visible but still subordinate to core transactional actions. The link uses the same clean sans-serif typeface as other nav items, sized for readability (around 14–16px) with 16–24px of horizontal padding on either side, and is vertically centered within the header bar, which is roughly 64–72px tall. Hover and active states may employ the brand aqua color for the text or an underline to signal interactivity, consistent with the site’s navigation style. On smaller screens, as the navigation condenses into a menu pattern (e.g., a hamburger), the help-center-link appears within the opened menu list, stacked with similar spacing and clear tappable area (at least 40px height per item).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Resides inside the header/navigation region and moves with it; the link itself does not have special positioning, but will follow any behavior the header adopts (e.g., if the header becomes sticky on scroll, the link remains visible within it).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site-logo-link is anchored at the far left of the top header bar, setting the starting point of the visual flow. It sits flush with the left inner margin of the content container (aligned with the left edge of the contact-form and hero content), and is vertically centered within the header’s height. The logo graphic itself is sized to be clearly legible without overwhelming the bar—typically around 32–40px in height—with some clear-space padding around it so it doesn’t feel cramped against the window edge or navigation items. Clicking the logo returns the user to the homepage, following standard web conventions. To its right, separated by a modest horizontal gap (24–32px), begins the row of navigation links including the help-center-link. The header background is a solid, light or neutral color that contrasts with the logo and complements the aqua + orange palette. On smaller screens, the logo remains left-aligned and retains priority visibility, while the navigation condenses or hides into a menu, ensuring the logo continues to anchor the design.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the header bar’s left side; it scrolls away with the header if the header is non-sticky, or remains visible if a sticky header pattern is adopted, but the logo link itself has no independent fixed or floating behavior.

name: Terms & Conditions
title: Terms & Conditions
description: Displays the dynamically loaded Terms & Conditions content and allows users to navigate to specific sections such as billing, cancellations, and delivery policies.

core_features: Load the Terms & Conditions content using getInformationalPageContent with a fixed pageSlug mapped to this route (e.g., pageSlug = "terms").
Render the returned title and body_html as the main legal document without hardcoding any legal text.
Display the last_updated value from getInformationalPageContent so users can see when the Terms & Conditions were last updated.

Build an in-page section navigation (table of contents) by analyzing the headings contained in body_html (e.g., H2/H3) so users can quickly jump to sections like billing, cancellations, and delivery policies if present.
Support deep linking to specific sections via a URL query parameter (e.g., terms.html?section=some-section-id) and automatically scroll/focus the corresponding section after content is loaded.
Allow users to switch sections via the in-page section navigation, updating the URL query parameter to reflect the current section without reloading the Terms & Conditions content.
Provide navigation back to the homepage when the user clicks the site logo, using a direct link to index.html.
Support navigation to the Privacy Policy page when the user clicks any cross-link to privacy.html that may be present within body_html.
user_workflows:
View full Terms & Conditions:
1. User navigates to terms.html (e.g., via the footer link).
2. The page determines the internal pageSlug value for this route ("terms").
3. The page invokes getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug = "terms".
4. After receiving the response, the page displays the returned title as the page heading, renders body_html as the main content, and shows last_updated in a metadata area.
5. The user scrolls through the document to read all terms.

Jump to a specific section from an external link:
1. User arrives at terms.html with a query parameter such as terms.html?section=<section-id>.
2. The page reads the section query parameter from the URL and stores it as the desired target section id.
3. The page invokes getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug = "terms".
4. After body_html is rendered and section anchors are generated, the page attempts to locate an element whose id matches the section parameter.
5. If found, the page scrolls the view to that element and applies a visual focus state (e.g., highlight or focus) to indicate the active section.
6. The user can then read that section directly and scroll to other sections as needed.

Navigate between sections using the in-page navigation:
1. User opens terms.html and the page loads content via getInformationalPageContent.
2. The page analyzes body_html to extract a list of section headings and associated ids (or generates ids from heading text) and builds a clickable section navigation list.
3. The user clicks on a section link (e.g., a heading that corresponds to billing, cancellations, or delivery policies if such headings exist in body_html).
4. The page updates the section query parameter in the URL to the id of the selected section, using history APIs (e.g., pushState/replaceState) to avoid full reload.
5. The page scrolls to the corresponding section element and updates the active state in the section navigation to reflect the current section.

Open Privacy Policy from the Terms & Conditions page:
1. The Terms & Conditions body_html returned by getInformationalPageContent may include one or more links to privacy.html.
2. The user clicks a link within the rendered body_html that targets privacy.html.
3. The browser navigates to privacy.html, where a similar mechanism loads the Privacy Policy content.
4. The user can then read the Privacy Policy without any additional API calls from the Terms & Conditions page.

Return to homepage from Terms & Conditions:
1. The user clicks the site logo displayed in the global header.
2. The page triggers navigation to index.html.
3. No additional calls to getInformationalPageContent are made on the Terms & Conditions page; the homepage handles its own data loading.
interactions: On initial page load of terms.html, the page reads the current URL to extract any query parameters (e.g., section) and then calls getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug = "terms".

After getInformationalPageContent returns, the page binds the response.title to the page heading component and to the document title, binds response.body_html to the content viewer component, and binds response.last_updated to a metadata display.

The section navigation component analyzes the rendered body_html (e.g., scans for heading tags) to build a data structure representing available sections (each with an id and label) without hardcoding any section names.

When the user clicks a section in the section navigation, the page updates the URL query parameter section=<selected-section-id> and scrolls to the corresponding section element in the content viewer.

When the URL's section query parameter changes (either on initial load or via navigation), the content viewer ensures that the corresponding section is scrolled into view and marked as active in the section navigation.
When the user clicks the site logo, the page navigates directly to index.html using standard link navigation.

When the user clicks any link within the rendered body_html that points to privacy.html, the browser navigates to privacy.html according to the link's href attribute; the Terms & Conditions page does not intercept or modify this behavior.

If getInformationalPageContent fails or returns incomplete data, the page displays a generic error state or fallback message (without hardcoded legal content) and does not attempt to fabricate terms; the user remains able to navigate away using the logo or browser navigation.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is controlled via the route (terms.html) and query parameters, without using localStorage or other client-side storage. The route terms.html is internally mapped to pageSlug = "terms" for calls to getInformationalPageContent. A query parameter section is used for section-level navigation: terms.html?section=<section-id>. On page load, the controller reads the section parameter and stores it as the target section. It then calls getInformationalPageContent({ pageSlug: "terms" }). When the response is rendered, the section navigation and content viewer use the section parameter to scroll to and highlight the matching section element whose id equals <section-id>. When the user clicks a section navigation item, the controller updates the URL's section parameter using history.pushState or history.replaceState (e.g., terms.html?section=<new-section-id>) without reloading the page, and updates internal state to mark the new section as active. No other state (such as previous scroll position or user preferences) is persisted beyond what can be inferred from the current URL.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading and rendering of the Terms & Conditions page. On load, it reads URL query parameters, calls getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug for this route, and passes the resulting data down to child components. It also manages URL-driven section navigation by synchronizing the section query parameter with the currently active section.

Invokes getInformationalPageContent with the internally mapped pageSlug value for the Terms & Conditions route and returns the resulting object (title, body_html, last_updated) to the controller. Handles loading and error states but does not render UI itself.

Displays the page title and last updated metadata based on the data returned from getInformationalPageContent. It also sets the browser document title to the returned title value so that the tab label matches the current document.

Renders the body_html returned from getInformationalPageContent as the main Terms & Conditions document. After rendering, it scans the content for heading elements to assign or confirm unique ids for each section and exposes a structured list of sections to other components. It also provides methods to scroll to a specific section id when requested by the controller.

Displays a list of sections (e.g., derived from headings in body_html) and allows the user to click to jump to a section such as billing, cancellations, or delivery policies if those headings exist. When a section is selected, it notifies the controller so that the URL's section query parameter can be updated and the content viewer can scroll to the appropriate section.
Represents the site logo in the global header. When clicked, it navigates to index.html, allowing the user to exit the Terms & Conditions page and return to the homepage.

Relies on standard browser behavior for links within body_html that point to privacy.html. Optionally, it can be used to track clicks or to ensure that any relative links to privacy.html work correctly, but it does not modify the link destinations or inject any hardcoded content.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same top-to-bottom stacking as the mockup: a full-width global header bar at the very top with logo and primary navigation (including the existing ORDER CTA), followed by a shallow, full-width legal hero band for the page title (terms-header) in place of the large marketing hero image, and then a full-width content band. Inside this content band, apply a 12-column grid: a left vertical strip for section navigation, a larger right column for the terms-content-viewer, and a generous right-side breathing gutter to echo the mockup’s three-column order/contact band.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same top-to-bottom stacking as the mockup: a full-width global header bar at the very top with logo and primary navigation (including the existing ORDER CTA), followed by a shallow, full-width legal hero band for the page title (terms-header) in place of the large marketing hero image, and then a full-width content band. Inside this content band, apply a 12-column grid: a left vertical strip for section navigation, a larger right column for the terms-content-viewer, and a generous right-side breathing gutter to echo the mockup’s three-column order/contact band.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly 10–12% of the initial viewport height for the global header, 18–22% for the terms-header band (a compact hero-style title area), and the remaining 66–72% for the content band within the first fold. Within the content band, allocate ~3/12 columns to the terms-section-navigation, ~7/12 columns to the terms-content-viewer, and ~2/12 columns as a right-side empty gutter. Vertical spacing follows the 8px base scale with 24–32px padding around each section, and 16–24px spacing between internal elements.
layout_strategies.content_density: Although the Terms & Conditions content is text-heavy, preserve the mockup’s moderately spacious feel by limiting text line length (max-width on the viewer), maintaining 16–24px spacing between paragraphs and headings, and using clear separation between navigation and content. Inputs are not present on this page, but similar spacing principles apply to the section navigation items (comfortable line height, 8–16px vertical padding per item) and margins between sections within the document.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the Z-pattern: the eye starts at the top-left global-logo-link, travels across the header navigation to the high-contrast ORDER button on the right, then drops down to the centered terms-header title band.
