From that center, the eye is guided slightly left to the vertically stacked terms-section-navigation links, then moves rightward into the main terms-content-viewer where the detailed legal text lives. Inline privacy links (handled by privacy-crosslink-handler) are highlighted in brand aqua, nudging attention deeper into specific related policies (e.g., Privacy Policy) without disrupting the primary reading flow.

layout.overall_description: The Terms & Conditions page adopts the same corporate, service-oriented structure as the marketing mockup. A full-width header bar anchors the top of the screen, with the logo on the left, main navigation across the center, and an orange ORDER button aligned to the far right. Directly beneath, a full-width legal hero band in a light, office-themed or subtly tinted background introduces the page with a bold “Terms & Conditions” heading centered horizontally, echoing the photographic hero’s visual weight but at a reduced height suitable for a legal page. Below this banner, a wide white content band stretches across the page, constrained to a central max-width container. Inside that container, the space is divided into a left vertical column with a clean, card-like list of section links (billing, cancellations, delivery policies, etc.) and a larger right column containing the main Terms & Conditions text. The right edge of the grid remains visually open as a gutter to maintain the airy, three-column feel from the original order/contact section. The text flows in a comfortable, readable column with generous line spacing and clearly separated sections, while inline links to related documents like the Privacy Policy appear in the brand’s aqua color. Overall, the layout feels professional and spacious despite the text-heavy content, maintaining a clear top-down visual hierarchy and a left-to-right reading flow anchored by the section navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-page-controller acts as the full-page structural wrapper, spanning 100% of the viewport width and the full page height. It establishes the vertical stacking order: at the very top sits the global header bar (containing the global-logo-link and primary navigation), directly beneath it the terms-header band, and below that the main content band that contains both the terms-section-navigation and terms-content-viewer. The controller centers the main content within a max-width (e.g., 1120–1200px) grid, applying equal left and right outer margins so the page aligns visually with other site sections. It enforces the 12-column grid within the main content band, managing responsive behavior so that on desktop the left navigation and right content sit side by side, while on smaller screens they stack vertically with the navigation appearing above the content viewer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page container that defines the primary vertical flow and responsive grid; it does not itself scroll independently but contains scrollable content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The informational-content-loader is positioned within the main content band controlled by terms-page-controller, centered over the area reserved for the terms-content-viewer. When active (while the Terms & Conditions are loading), it appears as a subtle overlay or inline loader centered horizontally within the main content column, approximately midway between the top of the content band and the visible bottom of the viewport. It does not cover the global header or terms-header band, allowing the page to still feel structured even during loading. The loader respects the same max-width as the content viewer, with generous whitespace (24–32px) above and below, and may slightly dim or skeletonize the content area beneath to signal loading status without introducing clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a transient, centered overlay or inline element within the terms-content-viewer region; fixed relative to the scrolling content (i.e., it scrolls away when the user scrolls).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-header occupies a full-width horizontal band immediately below the global navigation header. It spans 100% of the viewport width, with a height of roughly 18–22% of the viewport on desktop, visually echoing a hero but in a more compact form. The background uses either a softened version of the site’s office-themed hero imagery or a flat, very light neutral tone with a subtle aqua accent line, ensuring the text remains highly legible. Within this band, content is centered in the same max-width container as the main content. The main title “Terms & Conditions” is horizontally centered, with ample top padding (32–48px) and bottom padding (24–32px), and may include a short, one-line subtitle beneath, both set in clean sans-serif type. The text block spans about 60–70% of the container width, leaving comfortable left and right whitespace to maintain the airy feel. On scroll, the terms-header scrolls away with the page, maintaining the simple top-down flow from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section anchored below the global header; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-content-viewer sits in the main content band directly beneath the terms-header, aligned to the right of the terms-section-navigation. Within the 12-column grid, it typically occupies about 7 columns (approximately 60–65% of the total content width), starting a few columns in from the left to make room for the navigation and leaving a slim right gutter for visual breathing space. The top of the content viewer aligns vertically with the top of the section-navigation card, creating a clean horizontal baseline. Inside, the HTML content is constrained to a comfortable reading width (e.g., 640–720px) with 24–32px padding on all sides, and text is left-aligned with 16–24px vertical spacing between headings, paragraphs, and list items. Section headings (e.g., “Billing”, “Cancellations”, “Delivery Policies”) are visually distinguished with increased font size and extra top margin (24–32px), mirroring the clear section separation seen in the mockup’s form columns. The viewer scrolls as the primary reading surface, with the background remaining white and edge padding ensuring the content never feels cramped against the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page as the main vertical content stream; its width adjusts responsively (stacking below the section navigation on smaller viewports) while maintaining a max-width for readability.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The terms-section-navigation is placed within the main content band to the left of the terms-content-viewer, visually occupying a left column that reflects the leftmost column of the original three-column band. On a 12-column grid, it spans roughly 3 columns (about 25% of the content width). It appears as a vertically oriented, card-like panel starting just below the bottom edge of the terms-header, with a white background, subtle border or shadow, and 16–24px internal padding. The panel is aligned with the left edge of the main content container, leaving standard outer margin to the viewport edge. Navigation items (e.g., links to Billing, Cancellations, Delivery Policies) are stacked top-to-bottom, each with 8–12px vertical padding and clear 16–20px vertical separation between groups if sections are grouped. Links are left-aligned and use the brand aqua as the primary highlight color, with the active or currently viewed section indicated by a bolder weight or accent bar on the left. This panel is visually lighter than the main hero/title but clearly structured, inviting users to start reading from the left and then move right into the content viewer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and tablet, behaves as a vertically scroll-aware element that can become sticky: once the user scrolls past its initial position, the navigation panel remains fixed to the top of the viewport within its column until the end of the content band is reached. On smaller screens, it stacks above the terms-content-viewer and loses the sticky behavior to preserve vertical space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-logo-link is positioned in the top-left corner of the full-width global header bar, aligned with the same left margin as the main content grid to maintain consistent vertical alignment. It sits within the header’s vertical center, occupying a modest width (approximately 10–15% of the header bar) and providing a clear visual anchor in the Z-pattern. To its right, primary navigation items are laid out horizontally, with the high-contrast orange ORDER button aligned at the far right. The logo maintains sufficient horizontal padding (16–24px from the left edge of the content container) and vertical padding within the header to echo the spacious navigation density seen in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the global header at the top of the page; the entire header (including the logo) may stay fixed to the top of the viewport if the site pattern calls for a sticky nav, or scroll away as a unit if not. The logo remains the primary clickable element for returning to the homepage.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy-crosslink-handler governs inline links within the terms-content-viewer that reference related documents such as the Privacy Policy. Visually, these links appear inline within paragraphs or lists, following the normal text flow. They are styled in the brand’s aqua color with an optional subtle underline on hover or focus, providing a clear but unobtrusive contrast against the body text. Their placement does not occupy additional blocks of layout space; instead, they are interwoven with the legal text, typically appearing mid-line where relevant topics like data usage or privacy are discussed. Spacing before and after these links follows normal word spacing, ensuring the surrounding content remains visually cohesive and in line with the overall clean, flat typographic system.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely inline within the scrollable terms-content-viewer; they move naturally with the text as the user scrolls and do not alter the structural layout, only the interactive styling of specific text ranges.

name: Privacy Policy
title: Privacy Policy
description: Page that dynamically loads and displays the site's privacy policy content, including data handling details and user rights, using the informational content interface.

core_features: Load privacy policy content from the CMS using getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug derived from the route for this page.
Render the returned HTML body of the privacy policy, including headings, lists, and links, without hardcoding any policy text.
Display the policy title and last updated timestamp from the API response so users can see when the policy was last revised.
Support deep-linking to specific sections within the privacy policy via a URL parameter (e.g., section) that maps to HTML anchors within body_html.
Provide in-page navigation allowing users to jump to major sections (e.g., data collection, usage, user rights) based on headings parsed from the dynamic HTML content.
Enable navigation to the Terms & Conditions page via a cross-link inside the policy or a dedicated navigation element that routes to terms.html.
Allow users to return to the homepage by clicking the site logo, navigating to index.html.
user_workflows:
View the privacy policy:
1. User navigates to privacy.html via the footer link, direct URL entry, or other internal navigation.
2. On page load, the privacy-policy-loader component calls getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug set for the privacy policy.
3. The API returns an object containing title, body_html, and last_updated.
4. The policy-metadata-banner component displays the dynamic title and formatted last_updated timestamp.
5. The privacy-policy-viewer component renders body_html as HTML content.
6. The policy-section-navigator component parses headings within body_html and builds a section navigation list.
7. User scrolls or uses the in-page navigation to read relevant parts of the policy.

Open a specific section via deep link:
1. User clicks a link from another page pointing to privacy.html with a section query parameter (for example, privacy.html?section=<section-anchor-id>), or manually enters such a URL.
2. On page load, privacy-section-state-manager reads the section parameter from the URL.
3. After getInformationalPageContent completes and body_html is rendered, the component attempts to find an element whose id or name matches the section parameter.
4. If a matching element is found, the page scrolls to that element and may visually highlight it based on state.
5. The policy-section-navigator updates its selected state to reflect the active section.

Navigate from Privacy Policy to Terms & Conditions:
1. Within the rendered body_html or via a dedicated link area, the user clicks a link that targets the Terms & Conditions resource.
2. The click event is captured by a navigation handler that navigates to terms.html.
3. The browser loads the Terms & Conditions page, where a similar informational content flow occurs (outside the scope of this page).

Navigate back to the homepage:
1. User clicks the site logo in the header while on privacy.html.
2. The site-header component’s onLogoClick handler triggers navigation to index.html.
3. The homepage is loaded, and the privacy page is left.
interactions:
On page load (privacy.html):
- Trigger: Page initialization.
- Behavior: privacy-policy-loader invokes getInformationalPageContent with pageSlug corresponding to the privacy policy.
- Result: Page state is populated with title, body_html, and last_updated or an error state if the call fails.

On API load success:
- Trigger: Successful response from getInformationalPageContent.
- Behavior: Store the returned object in page state (e.g., privacyPolicyContent). Notify policy-metadata-banner, privacy-policy-viewer, and policy-section-navigator to render using this data.
- Result: User sees the policy title, last updated date, and full HTML content.

On API load error:
- Trigger: Error response or network failure when calling getInformationalPageContent.
- Behavior: Set an error state that privacy-policy-viewer uses to display a non-hardcoded generic error message template (without specific text) and a retry action.
- Result: User can click a retry control to re-trigger the API call via onErrorRetry.

Click in-page section navigation item:
- Trigger: User clicks a section entry in policy-section-navigator.
- Behavior: The component updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., privacy.html?section=<anchor-id>), scrolls smoothly to the corresponding anchor in the rendered body_html, and sets that section as active.
- Result: The relevant policy section is brought into view and may be visually distinguished by state (without specifying exact styling).

Deep-link handling for section parameter:
- Trigger: URL contains a section parameter when the page is opened or when the URL changes.
- Behavior: privacy-section-state-manager reads the parameter, waits until body_html is rendered, then attempts to find the matching anchor and scroll to it. If no match is found, no special scrolling occurs.
- Result: Direct linking to specific policy sections is supported without hardcoding any section names.

Click cross-link to Terms & Conditions:
- Trigger: User clicks a link to Terms & Conditions inside the rendered HTML (or a dedicated navigation item) recognized by the navigation handler.
- Behavior: The handler navigates the browser to terms.html.
- Result: User is taken to the Terms & Conditions page.

Click site logo:
- Trigger: User clicks the site logo in the header while on the Privacy Policy page.
- Behavior: site-header onLogoClick navigates to index.html.
- Result: User returns to the homepage.

Retry loading content:
- Trigger: User clicks a retry control after an error is shown.
- Behavior: privacy-policy-loader calls getInformationalPageContent again with the same pageSlug.
- Result: A new attempt to load the policy content is made.
page_functionality.state_logic: Routing and state rely on the URL and the response from getInformationalPageContent, without using localStorage or other client-side persistence. The core state elements are: (1) pageSlug: Internally fixed for this page to the value used by the CMS to identify the privacy policy. This slug is not user-editable and is used as the parameter to getInformationalPageContent. (2) section: An optional query parameter in the URL, e.g., privacy.html?section=<anchor-id>. When present, it indicates the target anchor within the policy content to scroll to. The privacy-section-state-manager reads this parameter on initial load and when the URL changes. When the user clicks an item in policy-section-navigator, that component updates the section parameter in the URL (e.g., using the History API) and triggers scrolling to the selected section. (3) loading/error/content state: Managed in memory by privacy-policy-loader. loading indicates that getInformationalPageContent is in progress; error indicates that the last attempt failed; content holds the latest successful response object. All child components react to these states to show loading placeholders, error templates, or the actual policy content. No static policy content, dates, or descriptions are hardcoded; all user-visible policy text is sourced from body_html and associated fields.

components.functionality: Provides the top-level page header, including the clickable site logo that navigates away from the Privacy Policy page when activated.

On initialization and on explicit retries, calls getInformationalPageContent with the internal pageSlug for the privacy policy, manages loading and error states, and exposes the resulting title, body_html, and last_updated data to other components.

Displays the dynamic title and last updated timestamp of the privacy policy based on the data returned by privacy-policy-loader, and reflects loading or error states as needed without hardcoding any textual content.

Renders the policy body_html as HTML, including headings, paragraphs, lists, and links, while handling loading and error states. It does not contain any static policy text; all displayed content is taken directly from body_html.

Builds an in-page navigation menu by parsing headings from the rendered body_html of the privacy policy and mapping them to their corresponding anchor ids. When a navigation item is clicked, it updates the URL’s section parameter and scrolls to the corresponding section.

Reads and writes the section query parameter in the URL, synchronizes this with the active section in policy-section-navigator, and after the HTML is rendered, scrolls to the element whose id or name matches the section parameter, if present.

Provides footer navigation for legal pages, including a link to the current Privacy Policy and a cross-link to Terms & Conditions (terms.html). The links are functional navigations and do not embed any textual policy content.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid, with the order/contact section indicating three equal-width columns (approximately 4 columns each) and full-width sections for header and hero.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same top-to-bottom flow as the marketing mockup: a full-width header bar at the very top, followed immediately by a hero-like metadata banner for the Privacy Policy (replacing the photographic hero but retaining the full-width band concept). Below that, a white content band visually analogous to the three-column order/contact area, but adapted into a two-column layout: a left in-page section navigator and a right main policy content viewer. The loader and URL state manager are non-visual/system-level and sit logically around the metadata and content areas. The page ends with a full-width footer containing legal links.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same top-to-bottom flow as the marketing mockup: a full-width header bar at the very top, followed immediately by a hero-like metadata banner for the Privacy Policy (replacing the photographic hero but retaining the full-width band concept). Below that, a white content band visually analogous to the three-column order/contact area, but adapted into a two-column layout: a left in-page section navigator and a right main policy content viewer. The loader and URL state manager are non-visual/system-level and sit logically around the metadata and content areas. The page ends with a full-width footer containing legal links.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain similar vertical proportions: header as a shallow strip (roughly 10–12% of initial viewport height), the metadata banner occupying a substantial band (about 25–35% of the initial viewport height), and the main content band (navigator + viewer) taking the remaining initial fold. Each region uses full-width backgrounds but content within is constrained to a central max-width grid (~1120–1200px). Sections are separated with 32–48px vertical padding, following the base 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep a moderately spacious feeling: the header is compact but not crowded, the metadata banner has few elements with generous whitespace around the title and metadata, and the main content band uses comfortable line lengths and 16–24px vertical rhythm between headings and paragraphs. The two-column main area spreads navigational items and long-form text so neither feels dense or overwhelming. Inputs are not used here, but paddings and gutters follow the same 8px multiples as the original form band.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Replicate the Z-pattern and downward flow: the eye starts at the logo on the top-left of the header, moves across the main navigation to the right-aligned orange CTA. From there the gaze drops into the center-left of the metadata banner where the 'Privacy Policy' title sits, then sweeps right across any secondary metadata (last updated, jurisdiction). Scrolling down, the eye first encounters the left-side section navigator (taking the place of the original left column form fields), then naturally moves right into the main privacy policy content (echoing the center form column). The bottom of the content leads into the footer legal links, creating a clear end to the page.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a professional, corporate web layout with clear vertical stacking of full-width sections. At the top, a white header bar spans the full browser width, housing the brand logo on the left, evenly spaced navigation items across the middle, and a high-contrast orange ‘Order’ button on the right, all aligned on a horizontal center line. Immediately beneath, a full-width privacy metadata banner functions like a subdued hero: it uses either a lightly tinted background (aqua or a softened office-themed image) and centers the 'Privacy Policy' title and brief metadata in a max-width content column, leaving generous breathing room above and below the text. Below this banner, a white rectangular content band sits against a very light gray page background, visually echoing the order/contact strip from the mockup. Within this band, a 12-column grid creates two main columns: a narrower left column with a vertical list of policy sections, and a wider right column with the actual privacy policy text, headings, and anchors. The content band runs downward as the page scrolls, with the left navigator staying aligned to the top of the viewport on desktop while the user reads through the right-hand text. Finally, a full-width footer with a slightly darker neutral background anchors the bottom of the page and centrally presents legal links, aligning with the site’s corporate tone and completing the vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The site header is a full-width horizontal bar pinned to the top of the page, spanning 100% of the viewport width and approximately 64–80px in height on desktop. It uses a white background with a subtle bottom separator line or shadow to distinguish it from the content below. Inside, a centered max-width container (around 1120–1200px) aligns content on a single horizontal baseline: the logo sits at the far left, occupying roughly 2–3 grid columns; the main navigation links are laid out horizontally in the middle, using equal spacing and about 16–24px horizontal padding per item; and the bright orange 'ORDER' CTA button is aligned to the far right, occupying about 1.5–2 columns and drawing the eye as the end point of the top Z-pattern. Vertical padding above and below the nav items is based on 8px multiples (roughly 12–16px), giving the header a clean, uncrowded look. Directly below the header, the privacy metadata banner begins, with no extra gap beyond a consistent 16–24px bottom padding within the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The header is statically positioned at the top of the document (not sticky by default), always occupying the full width of the viewport. As the user scrolls, it moves out of view, allowing the metadata banner and content band to occupy the main visible area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy loader is a non-visual system component that manifests only when content is being fetched or refreshed. When active, it appears immediately beneath the site header and above the privacy metadata banner as a slim horizontal loading indicator spanning 100% of the page width. This indicator is shallow in height, around 4–6px, and uses one of the brand accent colors (aqua or orange) over a neutral background. It visually aligns with the header edges and the metadata banner edges, forming a crisp line across the top of the content area. When loading is complete, the loader collapses to zero height and effectively disappears, ceding full visual attention to the metadata banner below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an overlayed, fixed-position bar directly under the header while active (so it remains visible during initial loading), then collapses and relinquishes its space entirely once loading is done, without affecting the layout of the surrounding sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy metadata banner is a full-width hero-like band located directly beneath the header (and loader, when active). It stretches across 100% of the viewport width and occupies roughly 25–35% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with a minimum height around 240–320px to maintain presence even on taller screens. Its background is either a softened, full-bleed office/water-themed image with a semi-transparent aqua overlay or a flat gradient in the brand’s aqua range, preserving the service-oriented, corporate aesthetic of the main site hero while dialing down promotional intensity. The content inside is constrained to the same central max-width grid (about 1120–1200px), aligned left within that frame. The primary 'Privacy Policy' heading is positioned in the left half of this container, beginning roughly one base unit (24–32px) below the top padding and occupying about 6–7 grid columns. Below the title, a short summary line and key metadata such as 'Last updated' and 'Effective date' are stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing, keeping the density moderate. On the right side of the banner, the remaining columns can carry secondary details (e.g., jurisdiction, data controller info) or remain visually lighter, ensuring the main focus stays on the title. Generous padding (32–48px top and bottom) ensures the banner feels airy and clearly separated from the main policy content below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page. The background image or gradient remains fixed to the banner’s area (not parallax) so that as the user scrolls past, the banner moves up and out of view, giving way to the main content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy section navigator lives within the main content band directly below the metadata banner, occupying the left portion of a two-column layout similar in spirit to the three-column order band from the mockup. The overall content band has a full-width, white background and is framed against a very light gray page background to clearly distinguish it as a primary reading area. Inside, a central max-width container is again aligned on the 12-column grid. The navigator column begins at the left edge of this container and spans roughly 3–4 grid columns (about 25–30% of the content width). It starts 32px–40px below the bottom edge of the metadata banner, aligned with the top of the main policy content to its right. Within this column, section links are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing, using clear typography and occasional group headings to avoid visual crowding. The overall height of the navigator column grows with the number of links, but on typical screens it occupies the full height of the viewport alongside the initial part of the policy text, maintaining close visual proximity so users understand the relationship between navigation and content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the navigator column behaves as a sticky side panel within the bounds of the main content band: as the user scrolls, it remains fixed near the top of the viewport with a top offset roughly equal to the header height plus a small margin (about 80–96px), ensuring it does not clash visually with the header. On smaller screens, the navigator reflows above the main content as a horizontally scrollable or collapsible element, no longer sticky, occupying the full width with a compact height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy policy viewer occupies the dominant right column of the main content band, directly beside the section navigator. Within the same central max-width container, it spans approximately 8–9 grid columns (around 70–75% of the content width), starting on the same horizontal baseline as the top of the navigator on the left. The viewer is rendered as a vertically scrollable text area integrated into the page (not a separate frame), with a white background matching the band and subtle padding: roughly 24–32px on the left and right, and 24px between major headings. The first visible elements are the policy introduction and possibly repeated title, anchored so they sit about 24–32px below the top inside padding of the content band, ensuring a comfortable gap from the metadata banner above. Section headings are clearly differentiated by larger type size and 16–24px top margin, with 12–16px between heading and following paragraph, maintaining the moderate density of the mockup. The overall column width is calibrated so line lengths stay around 60–80 characters on desktop, preserving readability. As the user scrolls down through the policy text, the navigator on the left remains visible (on desktop), reinforcing the connection between nav items and scroll positions. The bottom of the viewer leads into a small vertical gap (32–40px) before the footer begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content area that scrolls vertically with the page. It works closely with the URL state manager and in-page navigator for smooth in-page jumps and deep-link behavior, but its visual position remains anchored in the right-hand column of the main content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The privacy section state manager is an invisible, structural component that controls how the URL and scroll position stay in sync with the sections of the policy. It has no direct visual footprint and does not alter the spatial layout of other elements. Conceptually, it is attached to the main content band containing the section navigator and policy viewer, monitoring scroll positions and updating the URL hash or query parameters to reflect the currently active section. When a user navigates via a link or a shared URL, it ensures the appropriate section in the viewer is scrolled into view and the corresponding item in the navigator is highlighted, all without introducing any additional visible UI elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial. It operates in the background, tied to the document’s scroll and URL behavior, with no impact on dimensions, positioning, or stacking of visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer legal links component forms the final full-width band at the bottom of the page. It sits directly below the main content band once the policy viewer content ends, separated by a vertical gap of about 32–40px to clearly mark the transition from main reading area to site-wide footer. The footer spans 100% of the viewport width and uses a slightly darker neutral or muted aqua-tinted background than the main content band to provide visual contrast while maintaining the corporate aesthetic. Within, a centered max-width container (matching the header and content width) arranges legal links horizontally on desktop, with items centered or justified between left and right edges depending on the overall site pattern. Each link has 16–24px horizontal padding and 8–12px vertical padding, following the same spacing logic as the header navigation. On narrow screens, the links stack into 2–3 rows with generous vertical spacing to avoid clutter. The footer’s vertical padding (24–32px top and bottom) keeps it visually light and ensures it doesn’t overpower the main page content, while still clearly signaling the end of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block at the bottom of the document. It does not stick or float; it appears once the user scrolls past the main policy content and remains anchored to the page’s end.
